Daniela Feitoza

Velocidade do áudio:

Planejamento Pedagógico

Daniela Feitoza — Professional English — 43 Aulas

Nome
Daniela Feitoza
Idade
44 anos
Localização
São Paulo, SP (Berrini)
Profissão
IT Manager — SPF Group (Centauro/Nike)
Foco
SAP, Gestão de TI
Nível
A2+ (Pre-Intermediate)
Frequência
2x por semana, 60 min, online
Histórico
3 anos Lumine (grupo), concluído 2022
Objetivo
Conversational English para eventos internacionais e candidaturas a vagas internacionais
Distribuição
60% Speaking, 20% Listening, 15% Vocab & Pronunciation, 5% Reading/Writing

Jornada do Personagem

“Ai meu Deus, e agora?” — trava quando americanos se aproximam em eventos internacionais
↓
Profissional que cria conexões genuínas em eventos internacionais e se candidata a vagas globais com confiança

Promessa Transformadora: De “ai meu Deus, e agora?” a profissional de TI que faz networking internacional, apresenta projetos com clareza e se candidata a vagas globais sem medo.

Forças

  • Organizada e calma — perfil ideal para progressão estruturada
  • Prática — aprende fazendo, não só ouvindo
  • Background técnico forte — SAP, gestão de TI
  • Motivação clara — eventos internacionais e vagas globais
  • 3 anos de estudo prévio — base gramatical existente
  • Ambiente profissional internacional (Centauro/Nike)

Fraquezas

  • Trava em situações reais — medo de errar
  • Gap entre conhecimento passivo e produção ativa
  • Falta de prática oral espontânea
  • Vocabulário profissional limitado em inglês
  • Pronúncia com interferências do português
  • 2 anos sem estudo formal (2022-2024)

Currículo — 43 Aulas

Aula Tema Foco Linguístico Atividade Principal Dever de Casa
01Who is Daniela? Diagnostic + Personal IntroductionSelf-introduction structure: name, job, company, city, interests — present simpleWarm-up interview, diagnostic mapping, 60-second self-introduction recordingWrite 5-8 sentence paragraph introducing yourself at a professional event + audio
02Breaking the Ice — First Contact at International EventsGreeting formulas, small talk starters, question tags, present simpleRole-play at SAP conference, practice 5 natural openersListen to 2 podcast clips about small talk, write 5 openers
03Talking About Your Job — IT Manager at Centauro/NikePresent simple for job descriptions, company vocabularyBuild 3 versions of job pitch (10s, 30s, 2min)Record 30-second job pitch, self-evaluate
04Articles and English RhythmDefinite/indefinite articles, sentence stress, natural rhythmArticle exercises with own homework, pronunciation drillingRecord 10 sentences with correct articles, change Netflix subtitles to English
05Describing Your Company — Centauro, Nike Brazil & SPF GroupCompany description vocabulary, superlativesPresent company to imaginary foreign investorWrite company bio (6-8 sentences) + audio
06SAP World — Talking About Systems and TechnologyTech vocabulary, present simple for processes, SAP-specific termsExplain SAP workflow to non-technical colleagueWrite glossary of 10 SAP terms in English + examples
07Past Simple: Telling Stories That ConnectPast simple regular/irregular, time markers, narrative structureTell a professional success story using past simpleWrite a story about a project you completed + audio
08Networking in Action — Keeping the Conversation GoingFollow-up questions, active listening phrases, showing interestExtended networking role-play (5-minute conversation)Practice 3 conversation continuations with a friend
09Pronunciation Lab I — Sounds That Trip Up Brazilian Speakersth/r/l sounds, minimal pairs, connected speechMinimal pair drills, tongue twisters, recording comparisonsRecord 15 minimal pairs, listen and self-correct
10Meetings in English — Structure and Key PhrasesMeeting vocabulary, chairing phrases, agreeing/disagreeing politelySimulate a project status meeting in EnglishWrite meeting agenda + opening/closing phrases
11Present Perfect vs. Past SimplePresent perfect for experience/results, contrast with past simpleInterview about professional achievementsWrite 10 sentences: 5 present perfect, 5 past simple + audio
12Retail & Fashion Tech VocabularyIndustry-specific vocabulary, retail tech terms, Nike/Centauro contextPresent a retail technology project to stakeholdersCreate a glossary of 15 retail/fashion tech terms
13Expressing Opinions ProfessionallyOpinion phrases, hedging language, softenersDebate on technology adoption in retailWrite opinion paragraph on AI in retail + audio
14Future Forms: Plans, Predictions & IntentionsWill/going to/present continuous for future, planning languagePresent a project timeline for next quarterWrite 3 project plans using different future forms
15The Elevator Pitch — Presenting Yourself with ImpactPitch structure, power words, confident deliveryBuild and deliver 60-second elevator pitchRecord elevator pitch, get feedback from colleague
16Listening Lab I — Understanding Native SpeakersListening strategies, note-taking, gist vs. detailListen to TED Talk excerpt, summarize main pointsListen to 2 podcasts, write summaries
17Talking About Challenges — Problem-Solving Language in ITProblem-solution vocabulary, cause/effect connectorsDescribe a technical challenge and how you solved itWrite problem-solution case study from your work
18Conditionals I & IIFirst and second conditionals, hypothetical situationsDiscuss “What would you do if...” IT scenariosWrite 8 conditional sentences about work situations
19Social English — After the ConferenceCasual conversation, invitations, restaurant languageRole-play: dinner with international colleaguesWrite dialogue for a post-conference dinner
20Pronunciation Lab II — Stress, Intonation and ConfidenceWord stress patterns, sentence intonation, emphatic stressStress pattern drills, intonation exercises with recordingsRecord 10 sentences focusing on stress patterns
21Mid-Program Review + Speaking Fluency AssessmentReview of all structures, fluency measurementTimed speaking tasks, fluency assessment, goal reviewSelf-assessment journal + updated goals
22Email and Written CommunicationEmail structure, formal/informal register, common phrasesWrite and review professional emailsWrite 3 emails: request, follow-up, and thank-you
23Passive Voice: Professional and Technical ContextsPassive voice formation, agent by-phrase, formal toneRewrite active sentences to passive in IT contextWrite technical report paragraph using passive voice
24Talking About Data, Metrics and ResultsNumbers, percentages, trends vocabulary, comparativesPresent quarterly results using data languagePrepare a mini data presentation with charts
25Listening Lab II — Conference Talks and Panel DiscussionsAdvanced listening, speaker intent, note-taking strategiesWatch conference panel, identify key argumentsWatch SAP conference video, write key takeaways
26Cross-Cultural Communication — Working with AmericansCultural awareness, directness vs. indirectness, business etiquetteCompare Brazilian and American business communication stylesResearch 5 cultural differences in business settings
27Modal Verbs for Professional CommunicationCan/could/should/must/might in professional contextRole-play: giving advice and making requests at workWrite 10 workplace sentences using modal verbs
28Presenting Technology Projects to Non-Technical AudiencesSimplification strategies, analogy use, visual support languagePresent an SAP project to a non-technical boardPrepare simplified project presentation + audio
29Asking for and Giving DirectionsLocation vocabulary, prepositions of place, polite requestsNavigate a conference venue, ask for locationsDraw a map and write directions to 5 locations
30Reported Speech: Sharing What Was SaidReported speech transformations, reporting verbsReport on a meeting you attendedWrite meeting summary using reported speech
31Career Conversations — Talking About Your JourneyCareer vocabulary, accomplishments, future aspirationsJob interview simulationWrite your career story (past, present, future) + audio
32Handling Difficult Moments — When You Don’t UnderstandClarification phrases, buying time, paraphrasingPractice misunderstanding recovery in real-timeWrite 10 clarification phrases and practice with audio
33Vocabulary Building: Collocations and Word FamiliesCommon collocations, word families, academic word listCollocation matching and sentence buildingCreate 20 collocation cards with examples
34Conferences and Events — Before, During and AfterEvent vocabulary, scheduling, follow-up languagePlan and simulate attending an international conferenceWrite pre-conference preparation checklist + follow-up email
35Pronunciation Lab III — Polysyllabic WordsWord stress in long words, schwa sound, syllable reductionPractice technology and business polysyllabic wordsRecord 20 polysyllabic words with correct stress
36Listening Lab III — Understanding Business EnglishFast speech, idioms in context, business jargonListen to business podcast, decode idiomsList 10 business idioms with meanings and examples
37International Job SearchCV/resume vocabulary, cover letter structure, LinkedIn in EnglishReview and improve LinkedIn profile in EnglishWrite cover letter for international IT position
38Advanced Connectors and Discourse MarkersHowever/nevertheless/furthermore, linking ideas, coherenceBuild coherent argument using advanced connectorsRewrite a text adding appropriate discourse markers
39Storytelling for ImpactNarrative structure, hooks, emotional language, pacingTell a compelling professional story with impactPrepare and record a 3-minute professional story
40Consolidation Sprint I — Fluency Under PressureAll structures, spontaneous production, accuracy under pressureSpeed rounds: networking, presenting, discussing under time pressureSelf-record 3 timed speaking tasks
41Consolidation Sprint II — Full SimulationIntegrated skills, real-world simulationFull conference simulation: registration to follow-upWrite reflection on simulation performance
42Building a Sustainable English Routine Post-ProgramSelf-study strategies, resource curation, habit formationCreate personal English maintenance planSet up weekly English routine with specific goals
43Final Class — Full Review + Simulation + CelebrationAll program content, final assessmentFinal speaking assessment, celebration, certificateContinue your English journey!

Metodologia

1. PPP (Presentation, Practice, Production)

Cada aula segue o ciclo PPP adaptado: apresentação contextualizada, prática guiada e produção livre com foco em fluency.

2. Personalização Profissional

Todo o conteúdo é contextualizado no universo de Daniela: SAP, Centauro, Nike, eventos de tecnologia, gestão de TI.

3. Speaking First

60% do tempo de aula dedicado à produção oral. Daniela aprende fazendo, não ouvindo explicações.

4. Confidence Building

Progressão gradual de situações controladas para espontâneas. Cada aula termina com um “quick win” para reforçar confiança.

5. Simulação de Eventos Reais

Role-plays baseados em conferências SAP, networking events, reuniões com equipes internacionais e entrevistas de emprego.

Mapa de Personalidade

Idade
44 anos
Profissão
IT Manager — SPF Group (distribuidora Centauro/Nike) — São Paulo, Berrini
Stake
“Não vai conseguir criar conexões reais em eventos internacionais”
Citação-chave
“Ai meu Deus, e agora?” — ao ver americanos se aproximarem
Sonho
Criar conexões genuínas em eventos internacionais e se candidatar a vagas globais
Estilo de Aprendizagem
Prática — aprende fazendo. Energia calma e organizada. Precisa de estrutura clara e progressão visível.
Contexto Pessoal
Mora perto da Berrini (“Brooklyn paulistano”). Tem apartamento de praia em Ubatuba. Universo corporativo tech/varejo.

Bem-vinda, Daniela!

Seu programa Professional English começa aqui. Em 43 aulas, você vai transformar o “ai meu Deus, e agora?” em confiança real.

“Quero criar conexões genuínas em eventos internacionais e me candidatar a vagas globais sem medo de falar inglês.”

5 Frases de Emergência para Eventos

Essas frases salvam qualquer situação em eventos internacionais. Ouça cada uma e tente repetir em voz alta.

Hi, I'm Daniela. Nice to meet you.
Sorry, could you say that again?
What do you do?
That's really interesting. Tell me more.
It was great talking to you. Let's keep in touch.

Desafio: Ouça cada frase e tente repetir em voz alta antes da primeira aula.

Aula 01 — Pre-class

Who is Daniela? Diagnostic + Personal Introduction

Depois desta aula, você vai saber se apresentar com confiança em qualquer evento profissional.
0%
▼

Ao final desta aula, Daniela será capaz de se apresentar profissionalmente em inglês, incluindo nome, cargo, empresa, cidade e interesses.

Vocabulário — Personal Introduction Vocabulário

Veja a palavra, a tradução e o exemplo. Toque em Ouvir — o som ajuda a memorizar.

introduction·apresentação
“Let me give you a quick introduction about myself.”
manage·gerenciar
“I manage the IT department at SPF Group.”
based in·sediado em
“I'm based in São Paulo, Brazil.”
responsible for·responsável por
“I'm responsible for all IT operations.”
background·experiência/formação
“My background is in information technology.”
enjoy·gostar de/apreciar
“I enjoy working with technology and people.”

V1. Match the words to their meanings Vocabulary

Selecione a tradução correta para cada palavra.

introduction
manage
based in
responsible for
background
enjoy

V2. Complete with the correct word Practice

Preencha com a palavra certa. Use a dica em inglês se precisar de ajuda.

“Let me give you a quick about myself.”
Hint: a formal presentation of yourself to someone new
“I the IT department at SPF Group.”
Hint: to be in charge of a team or department
“I'm in São Paulo, Brazil.”
Hint: located or situated in a place
“My is in information technology.”
Hint: your education and experience in a particular field

P1. Complete the sentences Practice

Complete as frases de memória, sem olhar o vocabulário.

“I'm for all IT operations.”
“I working with technology and people.”
“Nice to you.”

P2. Choose the best answer Quiz

You are at an SAP conference and someone asks “What do you do?”. The best answer is:
A “I do computer things.”
B “I'm an IT Manager at SPF Group. We distribute Centauro and Nike in Brazil.”
C “I work.”
D “My job is very complicated to explain.”
Which sentence correctly uses “based in”?
A “I am based from São Paulo.”
B “Our company is based in São Paulo.”
C “I based in São Paulo since 2020.”
D “São Paulo is based me.”

P3. Put the introduction in order Practice

Organize esta apresentação pessoal na ordem correta.

?“I'm responsible for SAP implementation and IT infrastructure.”
?“Hi, I'm Daniela Feitoza. Nice to meet you.”
?“In my free time, I enjoy going to the beach in Ubatuba.”
?“I'm an IT Manager at SPF Group, based in São Paulo.”
?“My background is in information technology and I've been in this field for over 15 years.”

P4. Read aloud Speaking

Ouça a frase, depois grave você falando. O sistema vai comparar sua pronúncia.

Hi, I'm Daniela. I'm an IT Manager at SPF Group.
I'm based in São Paulo and I'm responsible for SAP implementation.
My background is in information technology.

T1. Think and respond Reflection

Imagine you are at an SAP conference in Orlando. Someone approaches you and says “Hi there! What brings you to the conference?”. How would you respond? Record your answer — there is no right or wrong here.
Sugestão de resposta
“Hi! I'm Daniela, an IT Manager from Brazil. I'm here to learn about new SAP solutions for our retail operations.”
Oi! Sou a Daniela, gerente de TI do Brasil. Estou aqui para aprender sobre novas soluções SAP para nossas operações de varejo.
Ou grave sua própria resposta

Survival Card — Lesson 1: Personal Introduction

1Hi, I'm Daniela. Nice to meet you.Oi, sou a Daniela. Prazer.
2I'm an IT Manager at SPF Group.Sou gerente de TI no SPF Group.
3We distribute Centauro and Nike in Brazil.Distribuímos Centauro e Nike no Brasil.
4I'm based in São Paulo.Sou de São Paulo.
5My background is in information technology.Minha formação é em TI.
Aula 02 — Pre-class

Breaking the Ice — First Contact at International Events

Aprenda a iniciar conversas com naturalidade em eventos profissionais.
0%
▼

Ao final desta aula, Daniela será capaz de iniciar e manter uma conversa casual em eventos profissionais usando small talk e question tags.

Vocabulário — Small Talk Vocabulário

Palavras essenciais para small talk em eventos.

networking·networking/fazer contatos
“Networking is the best part of any conference.”
attendee·participante
“There are over 500 attendees at this event.”
approach·abordar/se aproximar
“Don't be afraid to approach new people.”
keynote·palestra principal
“The keynote speaker was amazing today.”
first time·primeira vez
“Is this your first time at this conference?”
keep in touch·manter contato
“Let's keep in touch after the conference.”

V1. Match the words Vocabulary

Selecione a tradução correta.

networking
attendee
approach
keynote
first time
keep in touch

V2. Complete with the correct word Practice

Use a dica em inglês para encontrar a resposta.

“Is this your time at this conference?”
Hint: the ordinal number meaning before all others
“The speaker was amazing today.”
Hint: the most important speech at a conference
“Let's in touch after the conference.”
Hint: to maintain or continue something over time
“Don't be afraid to new people.”
Hint: to come near someone to start a conversation

P1. Complete the small talk openers Practice

“Great , isn't it?”
“Where are you ?”

P2. Choose the best opener Quiz

You are standing near the coffee station at a conference. The most natural opener is:
A “Tell me about your company now.”
B “Hi there! Great conference, isn't it?”
C “I don't know anybody here.”
D “What is your salary?”
A question tag turns a statement into a question. Which is correct?
A “This is a great event, is it?”
B “This is a great event, isn't it?”
C “This is a great event, doesn't it?”
D “This is a great event, aren't it?”

P3. Put the conversation in order Practice

Organize este diálogo de small talk na ordem correta.

?“That sounds great. Let's keep in touch!”
?“It really is! I'm Daniela, by the way. I'm from Brazil.”
?“Great conference, isn't it?”
?“Nice to meet you, Daniela! I'm Mike, from Chicago. What do you do?”

P4. Read aloud Speaking

Pratique estas frases de small talk.

Great conference, isn't it?
Is this your first time at this conference?
What brings you to the conference?

T1. Think and respond Reflection

You are at an SAP conference in Las Vegas. You see someone standing alone during the coffee break. What are 3 natural conversation starters you could use? Record yourself saying them.

Survival Card — Lessons 1-2

Personal Introduction
1Hi, I'm Daniela. Nice to meet you.Prazer em conhecer.
2I'm an IT Manager at SPF Group.Sou gerente de TI.
Small Talk
3Great conference, isn't it?Ótima conferência, né?
4What brings you to the conference?O que te traz?
5Let's keep in touch!Vamos manter contato!
Aula 03 — Pre-class

Talking About Your Job — IT Manager at Centauro/Nike

Construa 3 versões do seu job pitch: 10s, 30s e 2min.
0%
▼

Ao final desta aula, Daniela conseguirá descrever seu trabalho com clareza em diferentes durações.

Vocabulário — Job Description Vocabulário

Vocabulário para descrever seu cargo e responsabilidades.

oversee·supervisionar
“I oversee all technology projects in the company.”
lead·liderar
“I lead a team of 12 IT professionals.”
implement·implementar
“We implement SAP solutions for retail operations.”
distribute·distribuir
“SPF Group distributes Nike and Centauro products in Brazil.”
retail·varejo
“We work in the retail industry, specifically sportswear.”
infrastructure·infraestrutura
“I'm in charge of the IT infrastructure.”

V1. Match the words Vocabulary

oversee
lead
implement
distribute
retail
infrastructure

V2. Complete with the correct word Practice

“I all technology projects in the company.”
Hint: to watch over and direct work from a higher position
“I a team of 12 IT professionals.”
Hint: to guide or be the head of a group of people
“We SAP solutions for retail operations.”
Hint: to put a plan or system into action
“We work in the industry, specifically sportswear.”
Hint: the sale of goods to the public in shops and stores

P2. Choose the best answer Quiz

Someone asks “So what does your company do?” The best response is:
A “It's complicated.”
B “We're a distributor for Centauro and Nike in Brazil. I manage the IT side of things.”
C “We sell shoes.”
D “I don't know how to explain in English.”

P3. Put the job pitch in order Practice

?“My main responsibility is implementing SAP solutions for our retail operations.”
?“I'm an IT Manager at SPF Group.”
?“I lead a team of 12 IT professionals.”
?“We distribute Centauro and Nike products across Brazil.”

P4. Read aloud Speaking

I oversee all technology projects in the company.
We implement SAP solutions for retail operations.

T1. Think and respond Reflection

Record a 30-second job pitch. Include: your name, your title, your company, what the company does, and your main responsibility. Try to sound natural and confident.
Aula 04 — Pre-class

Articles and English Rhythm

Domine os artigos em inglês e desenvolva ritmo natural na fala.
0%
▼

Ao final desta aula, Daniela usará artigos corretamente e falará com ritmo mais natural.

Vocabulário — Articles & Rhythm Vocabulário

the (definite)·o/a (específico)
“The IT department is on the third floor.”
a/an (indefinite)·um/uma (geral)
“I need a new laptop for the project.”
stress·tônica/ênfase
“The stress falls on the second syllable: im-PLE-ment.”
rhythm·ritmo
“English has a different rhythm than Portuguese.”
syllable·sílaba
“The word 'technology' has four syllables.”
intonation·entonação
“Rising intonation at the end makes it a question.”

V1. Match the words Vocabulary

the (definite)
a/an (indefinite)
stress
rhythm
syllable
intonation

V2. Choose the correct article Practice

“I work at SPF Group.”
Hint: use this article when referring to a specific, known entity
“I need new server for the project.”
Hint: use this before singular countable nouns mentioned for the first time
“She is experienced IT manager.”
Hint: use this article before words starting with a vowel sound
“ conference starts at 9 AM.”
Hint: both people know which conference is being discussed

P2. Choose the best answer Quiz

Which sentence uses articles correctly?
A “I am manager at company.”
B “I am a manager at the company.”
C “I am the manager at a company.” (when talking about your specific company)
D “I am an manager at an company.”

P4. Read aloud with correct rhythm Speaking

Foque no ritmo e na pronúncia dos artigos.

I work at the SPF Group. It's a large distributor in Brazil.
The IT department has an important role in the company.

T1. Think and respond Reflection

Record yourself saying 5 sentences about your daily work routine. Pay special attention to using articles (a, an, the) correctly. Example: “I start the day with a team meeting. The meeting usually takes an hour.”
Aula 05 — Pre-class

Describing Your Company — Centauro, Nike Brazil & SPF Group

Apresente sua empresa para um investidor estrangeiro.
0%
▼

Ao final desta aula, Daniela descreverá sua empresa com vocabulário profissional e superlativos.

Vocabulário — Company Description Vocabulário

headquartered·com sede em
“SPF Group is headquartered in São Paulo.”
revenue·faturamento
“The company generates significant annual revenue.”
market leader·líder de mercado
“Centauro is the market leader in sportswear retail in Brazil.”
supply chain·cadeia de suprimentos
“We manage the entire supply chain for sportswear in Brazil.”
subsidiary·subsidiária
“Centauro is a subsidiary of the SBF Group.”
the largest·o/a maior
“Centauro is the largest sportswear retailer in Latin America.”

V1. Match the words Vocabulary

headquartered
revenue
market leader
supply chain
subsidiary
the largest

V2. Complete with the correct word Practice

“Centauro is the sportswear retailer in Latin America.”
Hint: the superlative form of 'large' meaning the biggest
“SPF Group is in São Paulo.”
Hint: having the main office located in a specific place
“Centauro is the market in sportswear retail.”
Hint: the company in first place ahead of competitors

P2. Choose the best answer Quiz

Which superlative is correct?
A “Centauro is the more big retailer.”
B “Centauro is the largest retailer in Latin America.”
C “Centauro is the most large retailer.”
D “Centauro is the more largest retailer.”

P4. Read aloud Speaking

Centauro is the largest sportswear retailer in Latin America.
We manage the entire supply chain for sportswear in Brazil.

T1. Think and respond Reflection

Imagine a foreign investor asks: “Tell me about your company.” Record a 6-8 sentence company bio. Include: company name, headquarters, what it does, size/position in market, and your role.
Aula 06 — Pre-class

SAP World — Talking About Systems and Technology

Explain SAP workflows and technology to international colleagues.
0%
▼

By the end of this lesson, you will be able to describe SAP modules and technology workflows to a non-technical colleague.

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary — SAP & Technology Vocabulary

implement
“We implement SAP modules across all departments.”
to start using a new system or process in a company
module
“The finance module tracks all transactions automatically.”
a separate part of a larger software system
workflow
“Our workflow includes three approval steps before any purchase.”
the series of steps needed to complete a work task
database
“All customer information is stored in our database.”
an organized collection of digital information
integrate
“We need to integrate SAP with our existing tools.”
to combine different systems so they work together
customize
“We customize every SAP module to fit our business needs.”
to change a system to match specific requirements
deploy
“We plan to deploy the new system next quarter.”
to make a system or software ready for use
upgrade
“We are upgrading our SAP version this year.”
to improve a system by installing a newer version

Stage 1.2: Match the words Vocabulary

implement
module
workflow
database
integrate
customize
deploy
upgrade

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context Grammar

Daniela works at SPF Group, where the IT team manages all technology systems. Every day, SAP processes thousands of transactions automatically. The finance module tracks every payment, and the inventory module monitors stock levels in real time. When a customer places an order on the Centauro website, SAP sends the information to the warehouse. The system does not make mistakes because every step follows a programmed workflow. Daniela customizes these workflows to match the company's needs. Does SAP integrate with other tools? Yes, it connects sales, logistics, and finance into one platform.

What does SAP do every day at SPF Group?
A It processes thousands of transactions automatically.
B It creates new products for customers.
C It replaces the IT team completely.
What happens when a customer places an order on the Centauro website?
A Daniela calls the warehouse manually.
B SAP sends the information to the warehouse.
C The system shuts down for maintenance.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — Present Simple for Processes Grammar

We use the Present Simple to describe how systems and processes work. These are facts that are always true.

FormStructureExample
AffirmativeSubject + verb(s)SAP processes data automatically.
NegativeSubject + does not + verbThe system does not crash often.
QuestionDoes + subject + verb?Does SAP integrate with your CRM?

Remember: he/she/it + verbs (The module tracks payments.)

Stage 1.5: Complete the sentences Practice

“We SAP modules across all departments.”
“The finance tracks all transactions automatically.”
“All customer information is stored in our .”
“We need to SAP with our existing tools.”
“We plan to the new system next quarter.”

Stage 2: Put the SAP workflow in order Practice

Organize the steps of an SAP purchase order workflow.

?“The manager approves the purchase order in the system.”
?“A store employee creates a purchase request in SAP.”
?“The finance module updates the payment records.”
?“SAP checks the inventory levels in the database.”
?“The supplier receives the order and ships the products.”

Stage 3: Read aloud Speaking

Listen to the phrase, then record yourself. The system will compare your pronunciation.

We implement SAP modules across all departments.
The finance module tracks all transactions automatically.
We need to integrate SAP with our existing tools.

Stage 4: Choose the best answer Quiz

A colleague from Germany asks: “How does your team handle inventory?” The best answer is:
A “We use a big computer for everything.”
B “We use the SAP inventory module. It monitors stock levels in real time and integrates with our sales data.”
C “Someone checks the warehouse every morning.”
D “I am not sure how inventory works.”
Your manager asks: “When will the new module be ready?” Which response is correct?
A “We deploy it yesterday.”
B “The team are deploying it now since last week.”
C “We plan to deploy it next quarter. The team is testing it now.”
D “Deploy is happening.”

Stage 5: Think and respond Reflection

A new colleague from Nike USA visits SPF Group and asks: “Can you walk me through how SAP works at your company?” Record a 5-7 sentence explanation. Include: what SAP is, which modules you use, how the workflow operates, and why it is important for Centauro.

Survival Card — Lesson 6

1We use SAP for all our operations.
2This module handles inventory management.
3The system integrates with our database.
4We are implementing a new workflow.
5Could you walk me through the process?
Aula 07 — Pre-class

Past Simple: Telling Stories That Connect

Narrate a professional success story in the past.
0%
▼

By the end of this lesson, Daniela will be able to narrate a professional success story using the past simple, describing a project she completed, the challenge she faced, how she solved it, and the result she achieved.

Warm-up — From Lesson 6

Callback

Last class you explained SAP systems. Use these Lesson 6 words again — but now, talk about a system you already implemented. Tap Listen to review.

workflow·the steps and order in which work is done
Prompt: “Which workflow did you improve last year?”
implement·to start using a new system or process
Prompt: “What system did you implement at SPF Group?”
module·one part of a larger system
Prompt: “Which SAP module did your team deploy?”

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary — Telling a Success Story Vocabulary

launch
“We launched the new system in June.”
to start something new, like a project, system, or product
solve
“We solved the problem in two weeks.”
to find an answer to a problem
achieve
“We achieved our goal on time.”
to succeed in doing something after effort
challenge
“The biggest challenge was the deadline.”
a difficult task or situation
deadline
“We met the deadline.”
the time when something must be finished
milestone
“Going live was a major milestone.”
an important point or achievement in a project
result
“The result was a faster system.”
what happens because of an action; the outcome
improve
“The new system improved our workflow.”
to make something better

Stage 1.2: Match the words Vocabulary

Launch
Solve
Achieve
Challenge
Deadline
Milestone
Result
Improve

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context Grammar

Last year, Daniela led a big project at SPF Group. The team wanted to launch a new SAP system, but the deadline was very tight. At first, they had many problems. Daniela made a clear plan, and the team worked hard every day. After three months, they solved the main problems and launched the system. The result improved the workflow for 200 people. It was a major milestone, and Daniela felt very proud of her team.

What did Daniela's team want to do?
A Hire 200 new people.
B Launch a new SAP system.
C Close the old company.
What was the biggest challenge?
A The team was too big.
B The deadline was very tight.
C There was no SAP system.
What was the result of the project?
A The project failed.
B It improved the workflow for 200 people.
C The team left the company.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — Past Simple for Storytelling Grammar

Use the Past Simple to talk about finished actions in the past. Regular verbs add -ed (launch → launched). Irregular verbs change form (make → made, have → had, feel → felt). For negatives and questions, use did + the base verb.

FormStructureExample
Regular (+ed)Subject + verb-edWe launched the system. / The team worked hard.
IrregularSubject + irregular formI made a plan. We had three months. I felt proud.
NegativeSubject + did not + base verbWe did not have much time.
QuestionDid + subject + base verb?Did you solve the problem?

Common mistake: "Did you launched?" → "Did you launch?" (after "did", always use the base verb)

Stage 1.5: Complete the sentences Practice

“Last year, we a new system.”
“The team very hard every day.”
“We the main problems in three months.”
“The deadline very tight.”
“We a clear plan together.”
“How you solve the problem?”

Stage 2: Put the success story in order Practice

Listen first, then organize the story from beginning to end.

?“We made a plan and worked together every day.”
?“In the end, we achieved our goal.”
?“Last year, we started a new SAP project.”
?“We solved the problems and launched the system.”
?“At first, the deadline was very tight.”

Stage 3: Read aloud Speaking

Listen to the phrase, then record yourself. Pay attention to the -ed endings.

Last year, we launched a new system.
The team worked hard and solved the problem.
We met the deadline and improved the workflow.
I felt proud because we achieved our goal.

Stage 4: Choose the best answer Quiz

At a networking dinner, someone asks about a project you finished. How do you start?
A “Last year, we launch a new system.”
B “Last year, we launched a new system.”
C “Last year, we are launching a new system.”
D “Last year, we did launched a new system.”
You want to ask a colleague about their project. What is correct?
A “Did you solved the problem?”
B “Do you solved the problem?”
C “Did you solve the problem?”
D “You solve the problem?”
You want to say the project was hard at the beginning. Which is best?
A “We didn't had much time.”
B “At first, the deadline was very tight and we didn't have much time.”
C “At first, the deadline is very tight.”
D “We not had much time.”

Stage 5: Think and respond Reflection

Tell the story of a real project you completed at SPF Group. What did you launch? What was the biggest challenge? How did you solve it? What was the result? Use the past simple and time markers (last year, at first, then, finally). Try to speak for one minute.

Survival Card — Lesson 7

1Last year, I worked on an important project.No ano passado, trabalhei em um projeto importante.
2At first, the biggest challenge was the deadline.No início, o maior desafio era o prazo.
3We made a plan and solved the problem together.Fizemos um plano e resolvemos o problema juntos.
4In the end, we achieved our goal.No final, alcançamos nosso objetivo.
5It was a major milestone for my team.Foi um marco importante para a minha equipe.
Aula 08 — Pre-class

Networking in Action — Keeping the Conversation Going

Follow-up questions, active listening e mostrar interesse para manter uma conversa viva em eventos internacionais.
0%
▼

Warm-up — Callback da Aula 7

Review

Na Aula 7 você usou o past simple para contar a história do seu projeto SAP: we launched, we solved, we improved. Hoje você vai para o OUTRO lado da conversa: quando alguém conta a história para você em um evento, como manter o papo vivo? (Today: the follow-up questions and reactions that keep a conversation going.)

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra, leia o exemplo e a tradução. Toque em Listen para ouvir.

Follow-up question -- a question that builds on the answer someone gave (PT: pergunta de continuação)
"She told me about her job, so I asked a follow-up question."
Small talk -- light, friendly conversation about easy topics (PT: conversa leve / bate-papo)
"We made small talk about the city before the meeting."
Have something in common -- to share an interest or experience (PT: ter algo em comum)
"We both love hiking, so we had something in common."
Get along with -- to have a good relationship with someone (PT: se dar bem com)
"I get along with everyone on the international team."
By the way -- a phrase to add or gently change the topic (PT: a propósito / aliás)
"By the way, how was your trip?"
Bring up -- to start talking about a new topic (PT: trazer um assunto / mencionar)
"He brought up the new project and we talked about it."
Look forward to -- to feel excited about a future thing, + noun or -ing (PT: estar ansioso por / aguardar)
"I look forward to working with you."
Mutual -- shared by two people (PT: mútuo / em comum)
"It turned out we had a mutual contact."
Reach out -- to contact someone, often first (PT: entrar em contato)
"Feel free to reach out if you have any questions."
Wrap up -- to finish or end something nicely (PT: encerrar / finalizar)
"Before we wrap up, let me give you my card."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a tradução em português.

Follow-up question
Small talk
Have something in common
Get along with
By the way
Bring up
Look forward to
Mutual
Reach out
Wrap up

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

At the mixer, Daniela met Mark. He said he worked in cloud security, so Daniela asked a follow-up question: "How did you get into that?" Mark explained that he started in support. "Oh, really?" Daniela said. "We have something in common — I started in support too. What do you enjoy most about it?" The conversation kept going. Later, Daniela brought up a new topic: "By the way, how do you know the host?" Before they left, Mark said, "We should stay in touch." Daniela smiled: "I look forward to it. Feel free to reach out."

1. Why did Daniela ask "How did you get into that?"
A It is a follow-up question to keep the conversation going.
B Because she wanted to end the conversation.
C Because she did not understand Mark.
2. Why does Daniela use "did" in "How did you get into that?"
A Because the action is happening now.
B Because she is asking about the past.
C Because "did" is always used in questions.
3. What is the function of "By the way" in the text?
A To say goodbye.
B To repeat the same topic.
C To introduce a new topic in the conversation.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — Follow-up Questions

GRAMMAR

Como construir perguntas de continuação (bilingue EN + PT).

TipoStructureExample
PresentWh- + do/does + subject + verbWhat do you work on?
PastWh- + did + subject + verb (base)How did you start?
Reply questionauxiliary + subject?"I work in IT." — "Oh, do you?"
Regra (PT)Toda pergunta precisa de um auxiliar: do/does (presente) ou did (passado). Depois de did, o verbo fica na forma base (get, não got). A reply question curta mostra interesse.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase completa.

"What kind of work you do?"
"How you get into IT?"
"By the , how was your trip?"
"It sounds like we have something in ."
"I look forward staying in touch."
"Feel free to out any time."

Stage 2: Put the Conversation in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de uma boa conversa de networking na ordem correta.

?Ask a follow-up question to learn more.
?Wrap up and exchange contacts.
?Introduce yourself and make small talk.
?Find something in common and bring up a new topic.
?React to show interest: "Oh, really?"

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a.

What kind of projects do you work on?
Em que tipo de projetos você trabalha?
Oh, really? How did you get into that?
Ah, é mesmo? Como você entrou nessa área?
That is interesting. Tell me more about that.
Que interessante. Me conta mais sobre isso.
By the way, it sounds like we have something in common.
A propósito, parece que temos algo em comum.
Feel free to reach out. I look forward to staying in touch.
Fique à vontade para entrar em contato. Estou ansiosa para manter contato.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação.

Someone says: "I work in data science." How do you keep the conversation going?
A "OK." (and stay silent)
B "Oh, do you? What kind of projects do you work on?"
C "Nice. Anyway, I have to go."
There is an awkward silence. What is the best move?
A "By the way, how do you know the host?"
B Look at your phone in silence.
C "I do not know what to say."
You both work in technology. How do you mention it?
A "You work in tech. I work in tech."
B "It sounds like we have something in common — I am in tech too."
C "We have the same in common job."
It is time to end the conversation politely. What do you say?
A "Bye." (and walk away quickly)
B "It was great to meet you. Feel free to reach out — I look forward to staying in touch."
C "I look forward to meet you again."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada.

Imagine you meet a new contact at an international tech event. Record a short conversation opener: introduce yourself, make small talk, and ask two follow-up questions. Try to use "by the way" and one reaction like "Oh, really?"

Survival Card — Lesson 8

1What kind of work do you do?Em que tipo de trabalho você atua?
2Oh, really? How did you get into that?Ah, é mesmo? Como você entrou nessa área?
3That is interesting. Tell me more about that.Que interessante. Me conta mais sobre isso.
4By the way, it sounds like we have something in common.A propósito, parece que temos algo em comum.
5Feel free to reach out. I look forward to staying in touch.Fique à vontade para entrar em contato. Estou ansiosa para manter contato.
Aula 09 — Pre-class

Pronunciation Lab I — Sounds That Trip Up Brazilian Speakers

O som do TH, a terminação -ed e os pares de vogais (live/leave) para soar mais claro e confiante.
0%
▼

Warm-up — Callback da Aula 8

Review

Na Aula 8 você aprendeu a manter uma conversa viva em eventos: follow-up questions, by the way, reach out. Mas de nada adianta a pergunta perfeita se a pessoa não entende suas palavras. Hoje é um laboratório de pronúncia: os sons que mais confundem brasileiros — o TH, a terminação -ed e o par live/leave. (Today: the sounds that make you clear and easy to understand.)

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra, leia o exemplo e a tradução. Toque em Listen para ouvir.

Vowel -- a speech sound made with an open mouth, like a or e (PT: vogal)
"English has many vowel sounds."
Consonant -- a sound made by blocking the air, like b or t (PT: consoante)
"The word stop starts with two consonants."
Voiced -- a sound with vibration in your throat, like the z in buzz (PT: sonoro)
"The b in big is a voiced sound."
Voiceless -- a sound with no throat vibration, just air, like the s in bus (PT: surdo)
"The p in stop is a voiceless sound."
Silent letter -- a letter you write but do not say, like the b in climb (PT: letra muda)
"The k in know is a silent letter."
Tongue -- the soft part inside your mouth that moves when you talk (PT: língua)
"For the th sound, your tongue goes between your teeth."
Minimal pair -- two words that differ by only one sound, like ship and sheep (PT: par mínimo)
"Live and leave are a minimal pair."
Mispronounce -- to say a word incorrectly (PT: pronunciar errado)
"It is normal to mispronounce a new word."
Tongue twister -- a sentence that is difficult to say quickly (PT: trava-língua)
"She sells seashells is a tongue twister."
Accent -- the special way someone pronounces a language; natural, not a mistake (PT: sotaque)
"You can keep your accent and still be clear."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a tradução em português.

Vowel
Consonant
Voiced
Voiceless
Silent letter
Tongue
Minimal pair
Mispronounce
Tongue twister
Accent

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

Daniela practiced with her coach, Greg. He showed her the th sound: the tongue goes between the teeth in think and thank you. Then they worked on the past tense. "We worked sounds like workt," Greg said, "with no extra vowel. But wanted ends in t, so it gets one extra syllable: want-id." Finally, Greg taught a minimal pair: live and leave. "It is normal to mispronounce these at first," he said. "Keep your accent — just be clear."

1. Where does the tongue go for the th sound?
A Between the teeth.
B Behind the bottom lip.
C At the back of the mouth.
2. Why does "wanted" get an extra syllable?
A Because it is a long word.
B Because it ends in the letter t.
C Because it is a voiced sound.
3. What does Greg say about Daniela’s accent?
A She must lose it completely.
B It is a serious mistake.
C She can keep it and just be clear.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — The -ED Ending

GRAMMAR

Os três sons da terminação -ed do passado (bilingue EN + PT).

Last sound-ed sounds likeExample
Voiceless (p, k, s, sh, ch, f)/t/ — no extra syllableworked, finished, stopped
Voiced (vowels, b, g, l, n, v)/d/ — no extra syllableplayed, lived, called
t or d/id/ — one extra syllablewanted, needed, started
Regra (PT)A terminação -ed quase nunca é uma sílaba extra. Só verbos terminados em t ou d ganham a sílaba /id/. Nos outros casos, é só um som rápido /t/ ou /d/ — sem vogal no final.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase completa.

"Two words with one different sound are a minimal ."
"For the th sound, your goes between your teeth."
"The z in buzz is a sound."
"The s in bus is a sound."
"It is normal to a new word."
"You can keep your and still be clear."

Stage 2: How to Practice a Hard Sound

Order

Coloque os passos para treinar um som difícil na ordem correta.

?Put the sound in a single word.
?Listen to the sound carefully.
?Use the word in a full sentence.
?Watch where the tongue goes.
?Say the sound slowly on its own.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a.

I think we should meet on Thursday.
Acho que deveríamos nos encontrar na quinta-feira. (foco no som TH)
We worked hard and finished on time.
Trabalhamos duro e terminamos no prazo. (foco no -ed, sem vogal extra)
I live here, but I leave at six.
Eu moro aqui, mas saio às seis. (foco em live vs leave)
Put your tongue between your teeth.
Coloque a língua entre os dentes.
I want to improve my accent and sound clearer.
Quero melhorar meu sotaque e soar mais claro.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação.

Which past verb has an extra syllable in the -ed ending?
A worked
B played
C wanted
How should "worked" sound?
A workt (one syllable, /t/ sound)
B work-ed (two syllables)
C worker (with an r)
"I ___ in Brazil" (I have my home here). Which word?
A live (short i)
B leave (long e)
C leaf
You did not understand a word. What is a good thing to say?
A Stay silent.
B "Could you say that word again, please?"
C "Repeat the word now."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada.

Record yourself saying three short sentences about your week. Use one th word (think, thank), one past verb with -ed (worked, finished), and one minimal pair word (live or leave). Speak slowly and clearly.

Survival Card — Lesson 9

1I think we should meet on Thursday.Acho que deveríamos nos encontrar na quinta-feira.
2We worked hard and finished on time.Trabalhamos duro e terminamos no prazo.
3I live here, but I leave at six.Eu moro aqui, mas saio às seis.
4Could you say that word again, please?Você poderia dizer essa palavra de novo, por favor?
5I want to improve my accent and sound clearer.Quero melhorar meu sotaque e soar mais claro.
Aula 10 — Pre-class

Pronunciation Lab II — Word Stress & Connected Speech

Acentue as palavras importantes, suavize as pequenas e ligue as palavras para soar natural, não robótico.
0%
▼

Warm-up — Callback da Aula 9

Review

Na Aula 9 (Lab I) você corrigiu sons isolados: o TH, a terminação -ed e o par live/leave. Agora suas palavras estão claras. Hoje é o Lab II: o ritmo do inglês — quais palavras são fortes, quais são fracas, e como as palavras se ligam para soar natural. (Today: stress the words that matter, reduce the small ones, and let your speech flow.)

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra, leia o exemplo e a tradução. Toque em Listen para ouvir.

Schwa -- the short, relaxed uh sound in unstressed syllables, like the a in about (PT: som neutro — o "átono" uh)
"The a in about is a schwa sound."
Content word -- an important word that carries meaning: a noun, a main verb, or an adjective (PT: palavra de conteúdo)
"In I love coffee, love and coffee are content words."
Function word -- a small grammar word, like a, the, to, of, or and (PT: palavra funcional)
"The and to are function words."
Weak form -- the short, soft way we say a function word, like to said as tuh (PT: forma fraca)
"In I want to go, to has a weak form."
Linking -- joining the end of one word to the start of the next so they flow (PT: ligação de sons)
"Turn it off links into one smooth phrase."
Contraction -- two words joined with an apostrophe, like I am to I'm (PT: contração)
"I am becomes the contraction I'm."
Emphasis -- extra force on the most important word in a sentence (PT: ênfase)
"Put emphasis on the word that matters most."
Unstressed -- said with less force, quieter and quicker than a strong word (PT: átono)
"Small words are usually unstressed."
Reduce -- to make a sound shorter or weaker (PT: reduzir)
"We reduce the small words to keep the rhythm."
Flow -- smooth, connected speech where the words run together (PT: fluidez)
"Good rhythm gives your speech a natural flow."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a tradução em português.

Schwa
Content word
Function word
Weak form
Linking
Contraction
Emphasis
Unstressed
Reduce
Flow

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

Daniela practiced rhythm with her coach, Greg. "Push the content words," he said, "the nouns, the verbs, and the adjectives. They carry the meaning, so make them strong." Then he showed her the small words: "The function words, like a, the, and to, are unstressed. We reduce them and use a soft schwa sound." Greg also taught two habits for good flow: use contractions like I'm and don't, and use linking to join the words. "Do not speak fast," he said. "Just put the emphasis on the right words."

1. Which words does Greg say to make strong?
A The content words: nouns, verbs, adjectives.
B The function words: a, the, to.
C Every word equally.
2. What happens to the small function words?
A They are removed.
B They are reduced, with a soft schwa sound.
C They become the loudest words.
3. Which two habits does Greg give for good flow?
A Speaking fast and loud.
B Pausing after every word.
C Using contractions and linking the words.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — Strong & Weak Words

GRAMMAR

Quais palavras são fortes e quais são fracas numa frase (bilingue EN + PT).

Type of wordRhythmExamples
Content words (nouns, main verbs, adjectives, adverbs)Stressed — loud and longreport, finished, happy, quickly
Function words (articles, prepositions, pronouns, auxiliaries)Unstressed — short and softa, the, to, of, and, I, you
ContractionsJoined — two words, one soundI'm, don't, we'll, it's
Regra (PT)Dê força às palavras de conteúdo (as que têm sentido) e reduza as palavrinhas de gramática. Use contrações (I'm, don't) e ligue as palavras para a fala fluir — assim o inglês soa natural, não robótico.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase completa.

"The important words that carry meaning are words."
"Small grammar words like a and to are words."
"I am becomes the I'm."
"Joining the end of one word to the next is called ."
"We the small words to keep the rhythm."
"Good rhythm gives your speech a natural ."

Stage 2: How to Build a Natural Rhythm

Order

Coloque os passos para dar ritmo a uma frase na ordem correta.

?Use contractions, like I'm and don't.
?Find the content words that carry the meaning.
?Link the words so they flow together.
?Say those words loud and long.
?Reduce the small function words.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a com bom ritmo.

I need the report by Friday.
Preciso do relatório até sexta. (forte: need, report, Friday)
I am not sure about that.
Não tenho certeza disso. (use a contração: I'm not sure)
Do not worry, I will call you.
Não se preocupe, eu te ligo. (contrações: don't, I'll)
We are working on it right now.
Estamos trabalhando nisso agora. (contração: we're)
Let me stress the important words.
Deixe-me dar ênfase às palavras importantes.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação.

In "I sent the report", which words are strong?
A I and the
B sent and report
C all of them equally
Which sounds most natural in a meeting?
A "I'm not sure, but I'll check."
B "I. Am. Not. Sure."
C "I am not sure but I will check it." (every word strong)
What is the weak form of "to" in "I want to go"?
A a soft "tuh" sound
B a loud, long "TWO"
C it is removed completely
Your colleague says you sound robotic. What should you do?
A Speak much faster.
B Stress the content words and reduce the small ones.
C Give every word the same force.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada.

Record yourself saying three short sentences about your work. Stress the content words, use at least one contraction (I'm, don't, we'll), and let the words flow. Speak with a natural rhythm.

Survival Card — Lesson 10

1I need the report by Friday.Preciso do relatório até sexta.
2I'm not sure about that.Não tenho certeza disso.
3Don't worry, I'll call you.Não se preocupe, eu te ligo.
4We're working on it right now.Estamos trabalhando nisso agora.
5Let me stress the important words.Deixe-me dar ênfase às palavras importantes.
Aula 11 — Pre-class

Present Perfect vs Past Simple — Talking About Experience

Diga o que você já fez na carreira e o que fez num momento exato — dois tempos, uma história clara.
0%
▼

Warm-up — Callback da Aula 10

Review

Na Aula 10 você trabalhou o ritmo do inglês: stress nas palavras de conteúdo, contrações e fala conectada. Agora sua fala flui. Hoje vamos dar a ela algo poderoso para contar: a sua experiência. Você vai aprender dois tempos verbais — o present perfect (para experiência, sem hora exata) e o past simple (para um momento terminado). (Today: say what you have done, and what you did, with confidence.)

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra, leia o exemplo e a tradução. Toque em Listen para ouvir.

Experience -- the knowledge and skills you have built from things you have done (PT: experiência)
"I have ten years of experience in IT."
Ever -- at any time in your life, used in questions (PT: alguma vez)
"Have you ever led a big project?"
Never -- not at any time in your life, the opposite of ever (PT: nunca)
"I have never worked abroad."
Already -- before now, often sooner than expected (PT: já)
"We have already finished the first phase."
Yet -- up to now, used in negatives and questions (PT: ainda)
"I have not finished the report yet."
Just -- a very short time ago, used with the present perfect (PT: acabou de)
"We have just launched the new system."
Since -- from a point in the past until now (PT: desde)
"I have been a manager since 2021."
Recently -- a short time ago, not long before now (PT: recentemente)
"I have changed teams recently."
So far -- up to this point in time (PT: até agora)
"So far, the project has gone well."
Accomplish -- to complete something important, often after effort (PT: realizar)
"I have accomplished a lot this year."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a tradução em português.

Experience
Ever
Never
Already
Yet
Just
Since
Recently
So far
Accomplish

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

Daniela talked about her career. "I have worked in IT for eight years," she said. That is her experience, with no exact time, so she uses the present perfect. Then she gave a finished moment: "I started here in 2017." That has an exact time, so she uses the past simple. "I have never worked abroad," she added, "but I have already led an international project. We have just launched a new system, and we have not finished the next one yet." So far, Daniela has accomplished a lot, and recently she has grown even more.

1. Why does Daniela use the present perfect for "I have worked in IT for eight years"?
A It is her experience, with no exact time.
B It happened at one exact moment.
C It is a plan for the future.
2. Why does she use the past simple for "I started here in 2017"?
A Because there is no exact time.
B Because 2017 is an exact, finished time.
C Because it is still happening now.
3. What does "we have not finished the next one yet" mean?
A The next project is already done.
B They never started it.
C It is not done now, but it is still in progress.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — Present Perfect vs Past Simple

GRAMMAR

Quando usar cada tempo verbal (bilingue EN + PT).

TenseWhenForm & example
Past simpleFinished action, exact time (yesterday, in 2017, ago)verb + ed / V2 — "I started in 2017."
Present perfect (affirmative)Experience / recent result, no exact timehave / has + past participle — "I have worked here for years."
Present perfect (negative & question)With never, yet, ever"I have not finished yet." / "Have you ever led a team?"
Regra (PT)Pergunte: eu digo exatamente quando? Se SIM, use o past simple (verbo no passado). Se NÃO — é experiência ou resultado recente — use o present perfect (have/has + particípio).

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase completa.

"All the things you have done in your career are your ."
"Have you managed a big team?"
"I have not sent the email ."
"We have launched the new system."
"I have been a manager 2021."
"I this job in 2017."

Stage 2: How to Choose the Tense

Order

Coloque os passos para escolher o tempo verbal na ordem correta.

?If there is an exact time, use the past simple.
?Think about the action you want to describe.
?If there is no exact time, use the present perfect.
?Ask yourself: do I say exactly when it happened?
?Add a signal word, like in 2017 or already.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a com bom ritmo.

I have worked here for eight years.
Trabalho aqui há oito anos. (present perfect — experiência)
I started this job in 2017.
Comecei neste emprego em 2017. (past simple — tempo exato)
Have you ever led a big project?
Você já liderou um grande projeto? (pergunta de experiência)
I have not finished it yet.
Ainda não terminei. (negativa com yet)
We have just launched the new system.
Acabamos de lançar o novo sistema. (resultado recente com just)

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação.

In a job interview, how do you say you have IT experience of ten years?
A "I have worked in IT for ten years."
B "I work in IT ten years ago."
C "I have worked in IT in 2015."
You want to talk about something you did at an exact time. Which is correct?
A "I have led the project last year."
B "I led the project last year."
C "I lead the project last year."
A colleague asks about your experience. Which question is correct?
A "Have you ever worked abroad?"
B "Did you ever worked abroad?"
C "Have you worked abroad yesterday?"
Your manager asks if the report is done. It is not done now. What do you say?
A "I did not finish it yesterday."
B "I have not finished it yet."
C "I have finished it yet."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada.

Record yourself telling your career story in four sentences. Use the present perfect for your experience (I have worked, I have led) and the past simple for exact moments (I started in 2017, last year I...).

Survival Card — Lesson 11

1I have worked here for eight years.Trabalho aqui há oito anos.
2Have you ever led a big project?Você já liderou um grande projeto?
3I finished the report yesterday.Terminei o relatório ontem.
4I have not finished it yet.Ainda não terminei.
5We have just launched the new system.Acabamos de lançar o novo sistema.
Aula 12 — Pre-class

Retail & Fashion Tech Vocabulary — Presenting a Project

Aprenda as palavras da tecnologia de varejo — e como apresentar um projeto de tech para stakeholders com clareza.
0%
▼

Warm-up — Callback da Aula 11

Review

Na Aula 11 você aprendeu a contar a sua história de carreira com dois tempos: o present perfect (experiência) e o past simple (momento exato). Agora você sabe falar do que já fez. Hoje vamos nomear o que você trabalha todos os dias: a tecnologia de varejo e moda — e como apresentar um projeto de tech para stakeholders. (Right words, clear pitch, confident manager.)

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra, leia o exemplo e a tradução. Toque em Listen para ouvir.

Catalog -- the full list of products a customer can browse and buy (PT: catálogo)
"The catalog shows every product in the store."
Inventory system -- the software that tracks how much stock a store has (PT: sistema de estoque)
"The inventory system shows the stock in real time."
Checkout -- the step where the customer pays and finishes the order (PT: caixa / finalização da compra)
"At the checkout, the customer pays in two seconds."
Payment gateway -- the service that processes a card payment securely (PT: gateway de pagamento)
"The payment gateway processes the card in two seconds."
Dashboard -- a screen that shows your most important live data in one place (PT: painel)
"The dashboard shows live sales to every store manager."
Analytics -- the study of data to find patterns and make smart decisions (PT: análise de dados)
"Our analytics show which products sell best."
User interface -- the screen and buttons the user sees and touches; the UI (PT: interface do usuário)
"The user interface is simple, so staff need little training."
Point-of-sale -- the terminal in a physical store where the customer pays; the POS (PT: ponto de venda)
"We integrate with your point-of-sale terminals."
Scalable -- able to grow with your business without breaking or slowing down (PT: escalável)
"The platform is scalable, so it grows with us."
Rollout -- the process of launching a product or system to all users, step by step (PT: implantação)
"The full rollout takes about six weeks."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a tradução em português.

Catalog
Inventory system
Checkout
Payment gateway
Dashboard
Analytics
User interface
Point-of-sale
Scalable
Rollout

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

Daniela presented her project. "The customer opens the mobile app and browses the catalog," she said. "The app connects to our inventory system in real time." Notice the words: inventory describes system, so the term is a compound noun — two nouns joined together, with the first one singular. "At the checkout, the payment gateway processes the card." Again, payment describes gateway. "The dashboard shows real-time analytics, and the user interface is simple. The platform is scalable, and the full rollout takes six weeks." Each part has a clear name, and that is what makes a pitch easy to follow.

1. In "inventory system", which noun is the main thing, and which one describes it?
A "system" is the main thing; "inventory" describes it.
B "inventory" is the main thing; "system" describes it.
C Both words are equally the main thing.
2. Why is "payment gateway" written with "payment" first?
A Because it sounds nicer that way.
B Because the describing noun comes first, then the main thing.
C Because "gateway" is a verb here.
3. What makes Daniela’s pitch easy to follow?
A She speaks very fast.
B She uses long, complex sentences.
C Each part of the system has a clear name.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — Compound Nouns (noun + noun)

GRAMMAR

Como formar termos técnicos com dois substantivos (bilingue EN + PT).

PatternHow it worksExample
noun + nounFirst noun describes; second noun is the main thing"payment gateway" = a gateway for payments
first noun singularThe describing noun is almost always singular"inventory system" (NOT "inventories system")
order mattersDescriber first, main thing second"user interface" (NOT "interface user")
Regra (PT)Em inglês a ordem é inversa do português: a palavra que descreve vem PRIMEIRO e no SINGULAR; a coisa principal vem DEPOIS. "Sistema de estoque" → "inventory system". Pergunte: que tipo de [coisa principal]?

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase completa.

"The customer browses the ."
"The app connects to the system."
"At the checkout, the payment processes the card."
"The shows real-time analytics."
"The platform is , so it grows with us."
"The full takes about six weeks."

Stage 2: How a Customer Buys

Order

Coloque os passos de uma compra no aplicativo na ordem correta.

?The app checks the inventory system for the stock.
?The customer opens the mobile app.
?At the checkout, the payment gateway processes the card.
?The customer browses the catalog and picks a product.
?The dashboard updates the sales data for the managers.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a com bom ritmo.

The app connects to the inventory system in real time.
O aplicativo se conecta ao sistema de estoque em tempo real.
At the checkout, the payment gateway processes the card.
No caixa, o gateway de pagamento processa o cartão.
The dashboard shows real-time analytics.
O painel mostra análise de dados em tempo real.
The platform is scalable and cloud-based.
A plataforma é escalável e baseada em nuvem.
The full rollout takes about six weeks.
A implantação completa leva cerca de seis semanas.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação.

A stakeholder asks how the app knows the stock. What do you say?
A "It connects to the inventory system in real time."
B "It connects to the inventories system in real time."
C "It connects to the system inventory in real time."
You want to say the platform grows with the business. Which is correct?
A "The platform is scalabel."
B "The platform is scalable."
C "The platform is a scale."
How do you describe the screen that shows live data to managers?
A "The dashboard shows real-time analytics."
B "The board dash shows real-time analytics."
C "The dashboard show real-time analytics."
A vendor says they will connect to your store terminals. Which term fits?
A "We integrate with your sale-of-point terminals."
B "We integrate with your point-of-sale terminals."
C "We integrate with your points-of-sale terminals."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada.

Record yourself pitching a retail tech project in four sentences. Name the parts (catalog, inventory system, checkout, dashboard) and end with one pitch word (scalable or rollout).

Survival Card — Lesson 12

1The app connects to the inventory system in real time.O app se conecta ao sistema de estoque em tempo real.
2At the checkout, the payment gateway processes the card.No caixa, o gateway de pagamento processa o cartão.
3The dashboard shows real-time analytics.O painel mostra análise de dados em tempo real.
4The platform is scalable and cloud-based.A plataforma é escalável e baseada em nuvem.
5The full rollout takes about six weeks.A implantação completa leva cerca de seis semanas.
Aula 13 — Pre-class

Expressing Opinions Professionally

Aprenda a dar a sua opinião com confiança e tato — frases de opinião, hedging e softeners para discordar com respeito.
0%
▼

Warm-up — Callback da Aula 12

Review

Na Aula 12 você aprendeu a nomear as partes de um projeto de tecnologia de varejo (catalog, inventory system, dashboard, payment gateway...) e a apresentá-las a stakeholders. Agora você sabe descrever o que tem. Hoje vamos um passo além: dar a sua opinião sobre essas decisões — de forma clara, suave e profissional, e discordar com respeito. (Clear opinion, soft tone, senior manager.)

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra, leia o exemplo e a tradução. Toque em Listen para ouvir.

Standpoint -- the position or angle from which you see and judge something (PT: ponto de vista)
"From my standpoint, the tool will save us time."
Perspective -- the particular way a person sees or understands a situation (PT: perspectiva)
"From my perspective, we should test it first."
Valid -- based on good reasons or facts; fair and worth listening to (PT: válido / justo)
"That is a valid concern, and we should look at it."
Reasonable -- sensible, fair and not extreme; based on clear thinking (PT: razoável / sensato)
"A six-week rollout sounds reasonable to me."
Soften -- to make a statement less strong or less direct, so it sounds polite (PT: suavizar)
"I soften my opinion when the team disagrees."
Hedge -- to say something with caution, without fully committing to it (PT: falar com cautela / sem se comprometer)
"I hedge when I am not certain: it might work."
Persuade -- to convince someone to agree with you by giving good reasons (PT: convencer / persuadir)
"Good arguments persuade the team to test the idea."
Hesitant -- unsure and slow to decide or speak; not yet confident (PT: hesitante / inseguro)
"I am a little hesitant about the deadline."
Downside -- the negative side or disadvantage of an idea or plan (PT: desvantagem / lado negativo)
"The downside is that it costs a little more."
Overstate -- to describe something as bigger, better or worse than it really is (PT: exagerar)
"I do not want to overstate the risk; it is small."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a tradução em português.

Standpoint
Perspective
Valid
Reasonable
Soften
Hedge
Persuade
Hesitant
Downside
Overstate

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

In the meeting, Daniela gave her opinion. "In my view, the new platform is a good idea," she said. Notice she opens with a clear phrase. Then she softened it: "It might take a little longer than planned." Words like might, could, perhaps and a little are softeners — they make her sound careful, not weak. When Marcus disagreed, she stayed polite: "I see your point, but I am not sure the team is trained yet." She never said "you are wrong". Remember: agree and disagree are verbs — we say "I agree", never "I am agree". A clear opinion, softened with respect, sounds like a senior manager.

1. Which phrase does Daniela use to OPEN her opinion?
A "In my view, the new platform is a good idea."
B "You are wrong about the platform."
C "The rollout takes six weeks."
2. What do softeners like "might" and "a little" do?
A They make her sound weak and unsure.
B They make her sound careful and professional.
C They are only used in writing, never speaking.
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "I am agree with you."
B "I agree with you."
C "I am agreeing you."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — Opinion Phrases, Hedging & Softeners

GRAMMAR

Como dar opinião de forma profissional (bilingue EN + PT).

MoveHow it worksExample
OpenStart with a clear opinion phrase"In my view, / From my perspective, / It seems to me that..."
Soften (hedge)Add might / could / perhaps / a little / I think when you are not certain"It might take a little longer."
Disagree politelyAcknowledge, then offer your view"I see your point, but I am not sure that..."
Regra (PT)agree e disagree são VERBOS: diga "I agree" / "I disagree", NUNCA "I am agree". Para soar profissional, ABRA com uma frase de opinião, SUAVIZE com might/perhaps quando não tiver certeza, e DISCORDE com respeito ("I see your point, but...") — nunca "you are wrong".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase completa.

"In my , we should test it first."
"The rollout take a little longer."
"I see your point, I am not sure that is the best option."
"I with the plan."
"That is a point, and it sounds reasonable."
"The is that it costs a little more."

Stage 2: Build a Polite Opinion

Order

Coloque as partes de uma opinião profissional na ordem correta.

?I see your point, but the team is not trained yet.
?From my perspective, the new platform is a good idea.
?It seems to me a small pilot would be reasonable.
?It might take a little longer than planned, though.
?So I agree, but let us not overstate the risk.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a com bom ritmo.

In my view, we should test it first.
Na minha opinião, deveríamos testá-lo primeiro.
It might take a little longer than planned.
Pode demorar um pouco mais do que o planejado.
I see your point, but I am not sure that is the best option.
Entendo seu ponto, mas não tenho certeza de que seja a melhor opção.
That is a valid point, and it sounds reasonable.
É um ponto válido, e parece razoável.
I do not want to overstate the downside.
Não quero exagerar a desvantagem.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação.

A stakeholder asks for your opinion. How do you open it professionally?
A "From my perspective, we should test it first."
B "You must do what I say."
C "I am agree with the project."
You are not 100 percent sure the deadline is realistic. How do you hedge?
A "The deadline is impossible."
B "The deadline might be a little tight."
C "The deadline is perfect, no problem."
A colleague says "We should launch now." You disagree. What is the polite reply?
A "I see your point, but I would test it first."
B "You are wrong, we cannot launch."
C "No. That is a bad idea."
The risk is actually small. How do you avoid making it sound too big?
A "This is a huge, terrible risk."
B "I do not want to overstate the risk; it is small."
C "The risk is the biggest problem ever."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada.

Record yourself giving your opinion in four sentences about adopting a new technology at work. Open with a phrase, soften one idea, mention one downside, and disagree politely with one objection.

Survival Card — Lesson 13

1In my view, we should test it first.Na minha opinião, deveríamos testá-lo primeiro.
2It might take a little longer than planned.Pode demorar um pouco mais do que o planejado.
3I see your point, but I am not sure that is the best option.Entendo seu ponto, mas não tenho certeza de que seja a melhor opção.
4That is a valid point, and it sounds reasonable.É um ponto válido, e parece razoável.
5I do not want to overstate the downside.Não quero exagerar a desvantagem.
Aula 14 — Pre-class

Talking About the Future

Fale de planos, previsões e compromissos — going to (planos), will (previsões) e present continuous (compromissos marcados).
0%
▼

Warm-up — Callback da Aula 13

Review

Na Aula 13 você aprendeu a dar a sua opinião com tato — abrir com "In my view", suavizar com "might / perhaps" e discordar com respeito ("I see your point, but..."). Agora você sabe dizer o que pensa. Hoje vamos transformar essa opinião em um plano: falar do que você vai fazer (going to), do que você prevê (will) e dos compromissos já marcados (present continuous). (Decide it, schedule it, predict it.)

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra, leia o exemplo e a tradução. Toque em Listen para ouvir.

Forecast -- a statement of what will probably happen, based on data (PT: previsão)
"The forecast shows higher sales next quarter."
Intend -- to have a plan or aim to do something (PT: pretender / ter a intenção)
"We intend to finish the rollout by June."
Anticipate -- to expect something and get ready for it before it happens (PT: prever / antecipar)
"We anticipate some delays during the launch."
Roadmap -- a plan that shows the steps and timeline of a project (PT: roteiro / plano de etapas)
"The roadmap puts the new system in the third quarter."
Scope -- the boundaries of a project: what it includes and excludes (PT: escopo / abrangência)
"A small pilot keeps the scope under control."
Allocate -- to give a share of time, people or money to a task (PT: alocar / destinar)
"We will allocate two engineers to the project."
Kickoff -- the first meeting that officially starts a project (PT: reunião de início)
"The kickoff is next Monday at ten."
Deliverable -- a concrete result or product that a project produces (PT: entregável / entrega)
"The first deliverable is a working checkout screen."
Contingency -- a backup plan for a problem that might happen (PT: plano de contingência / plano B)
"Our contingency is to keep the old system for a month."
Projection -- an estimate of a future number, like sales or cost (PT: projeção / estimativa)
"The projection is two thousand new users by December."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a tradução em português.

Forecast
Intend
Anticipate
Roadmap
Scope
Allocate
Kickoff
Deliverable
Contingency
Projection

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

Daniela is planning a pilot. "We are going to launch in two stores," she says — a plan she already decided. The kickoff is next Monday, and the team is presenting the results in May: these are fixed arrangements, so she uses the present continuous. She also makes a prediction: "I think the new system will save us time." Notice the forms: after going to and after will, she uses the base verb (launch, save) — never "will to" and never "going to launching". A good manager uses going to for plans, will for predictions, and the present continuous for arrangements with a time.

1. Why does Daniela say "We are going to launch in two stores"?
A It is a plan she already decided.
B It already happened in the past.
C It is only a guess with no plan.
2. Which form does she use for a meeting already in the calendar?
A will + base verb
B present continuous (am/is/are + verb-ing)
C past simple
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "I will to send the report."
B "I will send the report."
C "We are going to launching on Monday."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — Future Forms (going to, will, present continuous)

GRAMMAR

As três formas de falar do futuro (bilingue EN + PT).

FormUseExample
am/is/are going to + verbo basePlano já decidido / evidência clara"We are going to run a pilot."
will + verbo basePrevisão / decisão tomada na hora"It will save us time. I will send it now."
am/is/are + verbo-ingCompromisso marcado, com horário"We are meeting the vendor on Friday."
Regra (PT)Depois de will e de going to, use o verbo base (will send, going to launch) — NUNCA "will to" nem "going to launching". Use going to para PLANOS, will para PREVISÕES, e o present continuous para COMPROMISSOS já agendados.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase completa.

"We are to run a pilot in two stores."
"I think the new system save us time."
"We are the vendor on Friday."
"The is next Monday at ten."
"Our is to keep the old system for a month."
"The first is a working checkout screen."

Stage 2: Build a Project Plan

Order

Coloque as partes de um plano de projeto na ordem correta.

?The first deliverable is a working checkout screen.
