?We are going to launch the pilot in two stores.
?Our contingency is to keep the old system as backup.
?The kickoff is next Monday at ten.
?I think the pilot will work, and we are presenting the results in May.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a com bom ritmo.

We are going to run a pilot in two stores.
Vamos rodar um piloto em duas lojas.
I think the new system will save us time.
Acho que o novo sistema vai nos poupar tempo.
We are meeting the vendor on Friday.
Vamos nos reunir com o fornecedor na sexta-feira.
The kickoff is on Monday and the first deliverable is a checkout screen.
A reunião de início é na segunda e a primeira entrega é uma tela de checkout.
Our contingency is to keep the old system as backup.
Nosso plano de contingência é manter o sistema antigo como reserva.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação.

You already decided to test the tool. How do you announce the plan?
A "We are going to test the tool next week."
B "We test the tool yesterday."
C "We are going to testing the tool."
You look at the data and predict sales will grow. How do you say it?
A "Sales grew last month."
B "I think sales will grow next quarter."
C "Sales will to grow next quarter."
There is a vendor meeting fixed for Friday. How do you mention it?
A "We are meeting the vendor on Friday."
B "We will to meet the vendor on Friday."
C "We meeting the vendor on Friday."
A colleague worries the system could fail. How do you describe your backup plan?
A "There is no plan if it fails."
B "Our contingency is to keep the old system for a month."
C "We are going to ignoring the risk."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada.

Record yourself presenting a project plan in four sentences. Say what you are going to do, when the kickoff is, what you predict will happen, and your contingency plan.

Survival Card — Lesson 14

1We are going to run a pilot in two stores.Vamos rodar um piloto em duas lojas.
2I think the new system will save us time.Acho que o novo sistema vai nos poupar tempo.
3We are meeting the vendor on Friday.Vamos nos reunir com o fornecedor na sexta-feira.
4The kickoff is on Monday and the first deliverable is a checkout screen.A reunião de início é na segunda e a primeira entrega é uma tela de checkout.
5Our contingency is to keep the old system as backup.Nosso plano de contingência é manter o sistema antigo como reserva.
Aula 15 — Pre-class

Setting the Scene (Past Continuous)

Conte a história de um incidente: o que estava acontecendo (was/were + -ing) quando algo de repente aconteceu (past simple) — "I was testing the system when the server crashed".
0%
▼

Warm-up — Callback da Aula 14

Review

Na Aula 14 você aprendeu a falar do futuro — planejar com "going to", prever com "will" e marcar compromissos com o present continuous ("We are meeting on Friday"). Você planejou o piloto. Agora o piloto está no ar — e ontem à noite algo caiu. Hoje você aprende a contar essa história: pintar a cena com o past continuous (was/were + verbo-ing) e soltar o evento repentino com o past simple. (Paint the scene, then drop the surprise.)

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra, leia o exemplo e a tradução. Toque em Listen para ouvir.

Outage -- a period of time when a service is not working (PT: indisponibilidade / queda do serviço)
"There was a short outage during the night."
Crash -- when a system suddenly stops working (PT: travar / cair de repente)
"The payment service crashed at nine."
Glitch -- a small, temporary fault in a system (PT: falha pequena e temporária)
"It was just a minor glitch, not a real failure."
Alert -- an automatic warning that something is wrong (PT: alerta / aviso automático)
"The first alert came in at nine fifteen."
Monitor -- to watch a system closely to find problems early (PT: monitorar / acompanhar)
"The team were monitoring the dashboard all night."
Spike -- a sudden, sharp rise, like a jump in traffic (PT: pico / subida repentina)
"The traffic spiked, and then the service crashed."
Escalate -- to pass a problem to someone with more authority (PT: escalar / encaminhar para nível acima)
"We were losing transactions, so I escalated it."
Downtime -- the time when a system is down and not available (PT: tempo de inatividade / fora do ar)
"The downtime lasted twenty minutes."
Recover -- to bring a system back to working order after a failure (PT: recuperar / restabelecer)
"The engineer recovered the service in twenty minutes."
Root cause -- the real, underlying reason a problem happened (PT: causa-raiz / origem real do problema)
"The root cause was an old script using all the memory."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a tradução em português.

Outage
Crash
Glitch
Alert
Monitor
Spike
Escalate
Downtime
Recover
Root cause

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

Last night there was an incident. At nine, the team were monitoring the dashboard — a long action in progress, so Daniela uses the past continuous (was/were + verb-ing) to set the scene. Then a short, sudden event interrupted it: "While we were checking the database, the traffic spiked and the service crashed." Notice the pattern: the long background action is in the past continuous, and the short event that breaks it is in the past simple. The connector while comes before the long action, and when comes before the short event. Never drop "was" or "were": say "I was testing", never "I testing".

1. Why does Daniela say "the team were monitoring the dashboard"?
A It was a long action in progress (the scene).
B It was a short, sudden event.
C It is a plan for the future.
2. Which tense is used for the short event that interrupts?
A past continuous (was/were + verb-ing)
B past simple (spiked, crashed)
C present simple
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "I testing the system when it crashed."
B "I was testing the system when it crashed."
C "While we checked the database, it crashing."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — Past Continuous (Setting the Scene)

GRAMMAR

A cena longa e o evento curto (bilingue EN + PT).

FormUseExample
was/were + verbo-ingAção longa em progresso (a cena)"I was testing the system."
past simpleEvento curto e repentino que interrompe"...when the server crashed."
when + past simpleIntroduz o evento curto"...when the alert came in."
Regra (PT)Use o past continuous (was/were + verbo-ing) para a ação longa que estava em progresso — a cena. Use o past simple para o evento curto que a interrompe. while vem antes da ação longa, when vem antes do evento curto. NUNCA esqueça o was/were ("I was testing", nunca "I testing").

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase completa.

"I was the system when the server crashed."
"While we were monitoring the alerts, the outage ."
" I was reading the logs, I found the root cause."
"We were losing transactions, so I it."
"There was a short during the night."
"The engineer the service in twenty minutes."

Stage 2: Build an Incident Story

Order

Coloque as partes da história do incidente na ordem correta.

?We were losing transactions, so I escalated it.
?At nine, the team were monitoring the dashboard.
?While I was reading the logs, I found the root cause.
?While we were checking the database, the service crashed.
?The engineer recovered the service in twenty minutes.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a com bom ritmo.

I was testing the system when the server crashed.
Eu estava testando o sistema quando o servidor caiu.
While we were monitoring the alerts, the outage started.
Enquanto monitorávamos os alertas, a queda do serviço começou.
The traffic spiked and the payment service crashed.
O tráfego teve um pico e o serviço de pagamento caiu.
We were losing transactions, so I escalated it.
Estávamos perdendo transações, então eu escalei o problema.
While I was reading the logs, I found the root cause.
Enquanto eu lia os logs, encontrei a causa-raiz.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação.

You want to set the scene: you were checking logs and an alert interrupted you. How do you say it?
A "I was checking the logs when the alert came in."
B "I checked the logs when the alert coming in."
C "I check the logs when the alert came in."
A long action interrupted by a short one. Which connector goes before the long action?
A "when" + past continuous
B "while" + past continuous
C "after" + past simple
The service stopped working suddenly. How do you describe it?
A "The payment service crashed."
B "The payment service was crashing all the time."
C "The payment service is crash."
You found the real reason behind the problem. How do you report it?
A "We do not know why it happened."
B "The root cause was an old script using all the memory."
C "The problem will happen again next week."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada.

Record yourself telling the story of a real incident in four sentences. Set the scene (was/were + -ing), describe the sudden event (past simple), say what you did, and explain the root cause.

Survival Card — Lesson 15

1I was testing the system when the server crashed.Eu estava testando o sistema quando o servidor caiu.
2While we were monitoring the alerts, the outage started.Enquanto monitorávamos os alertas, a queda do serviço começou.
3The traffic spiked and the payment service crashed.O tráfego teve um pico e o serviço de pagamento caiu.
4We were losing transactions, so I escalated it.Estávamos perdendo transações, então eu escalei o problema.
5While I was reading the logs, I found the root cause.Enquanto eu lia os logs, encontrei a causa-raiz.
Aula 16 — Pre-class

Sequencing the Past (Past Perfect)

Coloque a história em ordem: o que já tinha acontecido antes de outro evento passado (had + past participle) + sequenciadores first/then/finally — "I had already escalated it when the engineer arrived".
0%
▼

Warm-up — Callback da Aula 15

Review

Na Aula 15 você aprendeu a montar a cena de um incidente com o past continuous ("I was testing the system when the server crashed"). Agora você coloca dois eventos passados em ordem: qual aconteceu primeiro? Para a ação mais antiga, você usa o past perfect (had + past participle): "I had escalated it when he arrived". E usa sequenciadores (first, then, after that, finally) para deixar a história clara. (Show what happened first, then what came next.)

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra, leia o exemplo e a tradução. Toque em Listen para ouvir.

Postmortem -- a review meeting after an incident to learn what happened (PT: análise pós-incidente / retrospectiva)
"In the postmortem, we reviewed the full timeline."
Timeline -- the events of an incident listed in order, with times (PT: linha do tempo / cronologia)
"Here is the full timeline of last night."
Culprit -- the exact thing or person that caused the problem (PT: o culpado / a causa exata)
"The culprit was an old background script."
Rollback -- a return of a system to a previous, working version (PT: reversão / volta à versão anterior)
"We started the rollback to the old version."
Trigger -- to cause something to start or happen (PT: disparar / desencadear)
"The old script had triggered the memory problem."
Assess -- to judge or measure how serious a situation is (PT: avaliar / mensurar)
"We had assessed the impact before he arrived."
Restore -- to bring a service back to its normal, working state (PT: restaurar / restabelecer)
"Finally, we restored the service."
Mitigate -- to make a risk or a negative impact smaller (PT: mitigar / reduzir o impacto)
"We have a plan to mitigate the risk."
Pinpoint -- to find the exact cause or location of a problem (PT: identificar com precisão / localizar)
"I had pinpointed the culprit before the meeting."
Aftermath -- the period and effects right after a bad event (PT: rescaldo / período após o evento)
"In the aftermath, support had a busy hour."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a tradução em português.

Postmortem
Timeline
Culprit
Rollback
Trigger
Assess
Restore
Mitigate
Pinpoint
Aftermath

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

In the postmortem, Daniela told the story in order. First, the traffic spiked. Then, the service crashed. By the time the engineer arrived, she had already escalated the issue — the escalation happened before his arrival, so she uses the past perfect (had + past participle) for the earlier action and the past simple (arrived) for the later one. Before the meeting started, she had pinpointed the culprit: an old script had triggered the memory problem. Finally, the team mitigated the risk. Remember: the past perfect is always had + past participle, never "has", and sequencers (first, then, after that, finally) make the order clear.

1. Why does Daniela say "she had already escalated the issue"?
A The escalation happened before the engineer arrived.
B The escalation happened after he arrived.
C It is a plan for the future.
2. Which tense marks the earlier of two past actions?
A past simple
B past perfect (had + past participle)
C present perfect
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "We has restored the service before he logged in."
B "We had restored the service before he logged in."
C "We restore the service before he logged in."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — Past Perfect & Sequencers

GRAMMAR

A ação mais antiga e os sequenciadores (bilingue EN + PT).

FormUseExample
had + past participleA ação mais antiga de duas no passado"I had escalated it."
past simpleA ação mais recente"...when he arrived."
first, then, after that, finallySequenciadores que ordenam a história"First it spiked, then it crashed."
Regra (PT)Use o past perfect (had + past participle) para a ação que aconteceu PRIMEIRO; use o past simple para a ação mais recente. Os sequenciadores (first, then, after that, finally) deixam a ordem clara. O past perfect é SEMPRE had, nunca "has".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase completa.

"By the time he arrived, I had already the issue."
"By the time he logged in, we restored the service."
"First it spiked, it crashed, and after that I escalated it."
"Before the meeting, I had the culprit."
"An old script had the memory problem."
", we made a plan to mitigate the risk."

Stage 2: Build the Postmortem Timeline

Order

Coloque os eventos do incidente na ordem em que aconteceram.

?After that, we started the rollback to the old version.
?First, the traffic spiked at nine.
?Before the meeting, I had pinpointed the culprit.
?Then, the service crashed and I escalated it.
?Finally, we restored the service and made a plan to mitigate the risk.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a com bom ritmo.

I had already escalated it when the engineer arrived.
Eu já tinha escalado o problema quando o engenheiro chegou.
By the time he logged in, we had restored the service.
Quando ele entrou no sistema, nós já tínhamos restaurado o serviço.
First it spiked, then it crashed, and after that I escalated it.
Primeiro teve um pico, depois caiu, e em seguida eu escalei.
Before the meeting, I had pinpointed the culprit.
Antes da reunião, eu já tinha identificado o culpado.
An old script had triggered the memory problem.
Um script antigo tinha disparado o problema de memória.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação.

The escalation happened before the engineer arrived. How do you say it?
A "I had escalated it when the engineer arrived."
B "I escalate it when the engineer arrived."
C "I has escalated it when the engineer arrived."
You want to put three events in order. Which sentence is best?
A "It crashed, it spiked, I escalated."
B "First it spiked, then it crashed, and after that I escalated it."
C "Finally it spiked, first it crashed."
You found the exact cause of the incident. How do you report it?
A "I had pinpointed the culprit before the meeting."
B "I pinpoint the culprit yesterday meeting."
C "The culprit will happen next week."
You reduced the risk after the incident. How do you describe it?
A "We ignored the risk completely."
B "Finally, we made a plan to mitigate the risk."
C "We are going to crash the service again."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada.

Record yourself presenting the postmortem of a real incident in four sentences. Order the events with first, then and finally, and use the past perfect (had + done) for what had already happened.

Survival Card — Lesson 16

1I had already escalated it when the engineer arrived.Eu já tinha escalado o problema quando o engenheiro chegou.
2By the time he logged in, we had restored the service.Quando ele entrou no sistema, nós já tínhamos restaurado o serviço.
3First it spiked, then it crashed, and after that I escalated it.Primeiro teve um pico, depois caiu, e em seguida eu escalei.
4Before the meeting, I had pinpointed the culprit.Antes da reunião, eu já tinha identificado o culpado.
5Finally, we made a plan to mitigate the risk.Por fim, fizemos um plano para mitigar o risco.
Aula 17 — Pre-class

Comparing Then and Now (used to / would)

Compare passado e presente: como as coisas costumavam ser (used to + verbo base, para hábitos e estados) e ações repetidas (would + verbo base) — "I used to work on call, but now we have a rotation".
0%
▼

Warm-up — Callback da Aula 16

Review

Na Aula 16 você aprendeu a ordenar uma história com o past perfect ("By the time he arrived, I had escalated it") + sequenciadores. Agora você compara o passado com o presente: como algo costumava ser. Para hábitos e estados do passado que mudaram, use used to + verbo base ("I used to work on call"). Para ações repetidas do passado, use would + verbo base ("the system would crash"). E feche com but now para o contraste. (Say how it used to be, then how it is today.)

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra, leia o exemplo e a tradução. Toque em Listen para ouvir.

Legacy -- an old system or technology that is still in use (PT: sistema legado / antigo em uso)
"The legacy system used to crash every night."
Manual -- done by hand, without automation (PT: manual / feito à mão)
"We used to do the deployment manually."
Rotation -- a system where people take turns doing a duty (PT: rodízio / escala)
"Now we have a rotation, so no one is always on call."
Outdated -- too old to be useful or correct anymore (PT: ultrapassado / desatualizado)
"The outdated scripts are gone now."
Streamline -- to make a process simpler and more efficient (PT: simplificar / otimizar)
"We streamlined the process and removed extra steps."
Backlog -- a large amount of unfinished work that has built up (PT: fila de pendências / acúmulo)
"We used to have a huge backlog of tickets."
Handover -- passing work or responsibility from one shift to the next (PT: passagem de turno / transferência)
"The handover between shifts used to be a mess."
Tedious -- boring and tiring because it is slow and repetitive (PT: tedioso / maçante)
"The old process was slow and tedious."
Automate -- to make a machine do a task without a person (PT: automatizar)
"Now the deployment is automated."
Overhaul -- a complete change or renovation of a system (PT: revisão completa / reformulação)
"We did a full overhaul of the old code."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a tradução em português.

Legacy
Manual
Rotation
Outdated
Streamline
Backlog
Handover
Tedious
Automate
Overhaul

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

Two years ago, the team worked very differently. They used to firefight every night — a past habit, so Daniela uses used to + base verb. The legacy system would crash, and one person would fix it alone: these are repeated past actions, so she uses would + base verb. The process used to be slow and tedious — this is a past state, so she must use used to be, never "would be". To show the change, she adds but now: "We used to deploy manually, but now it is automated." Remember: "used to" covers habits and states; "would" covers only repeated actions, not states.

1. Why does Daniela say "they used to firefight every night"?
A It was a past habit that is not true now.
B It is a plan for the future.
C It is still true today.
2. Why does she use "used to be" and not "would be" for the process?
A Because it was a repeated action.
B Because it is a past state, and "would" is not used for states.
C Because it is in the future.
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "I would have an old laptop."
B "I used to have an old laptop."
C "I use to work on call."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — used to / would & but now

GRAMMAR

Hábitos e estados do passado vs ações repetidas (bilingue EN + PT).

FormUseExample
used to + verbo baseHábito OU estado do passado que mudou"I used to work on call."
would + verbo baseAção repetida do passado (não para estados)"The system would crash every night."
but now + presenteContraste com o presente"...but now it is automated."
Regra (PT)Use used to + verbo base para hábitos E estados do passado. Use would + verbo base APENAS para ações repetidas (rotinas) — NUNCA para estados (não se diz "I would have"; o certo é "I used to have"). Sempre escreva used to com o -d. Feche com but now para o contraste.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase completa.

"I to work on call, but now we have a rotation."
"Every night, the legacy system crash."
"The process used to slow and tedious."
"We used to deploy manually, but now it is ."
"We did a full of the old code."
"The scripts are gone now."

Stage 2: Build a Then-and-Now Story

Order

Coloque a história da mudança do time na ordem correta.

?But now we have a rotation, so no one burns out.
?We used to firefight every night, on call alone.
?We streamlined the process and automated the deployment.
?The legacy system would crash, and the process was tedious.
?Finally, we did a full overhaul, and the outdated scripts are gone.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a com bom ritmo.

I used to work on call, but now we have a rotation.
Eu costumava ficar de plantão, mas agora temos um rodízio.
Every night, the legacy system would crash.
Toda noite, o sistema legado caia.
The process used to be slow and tedious.
O processo costumava ser lento e tedioso.
We used to deploy manually, but now it is automated.
Nós costávamos implantar manualmente, mas agora é automatizado.
We did a full overhaul, and the outdated scripts are gone.
Fizemos uma reformulação completa, e os scripts ultrapassados sumiram.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação.

You want to describe a past habit that has changed. How do you say it?
A "I used to work on call, but now we have a rotation."
B "I work on call, but now we had a rotation."
C "I use to work on call."
A repeated past action: the system crashed again and again. How do you say it?
A "The system would be old."
B "The system would crash every night."
C "The system will crash every night."
A past state: the laptop was old. Which is correct?
A "I used to have an old laptop."
B "I would have an old laptop."
C "I would had an old laptop."
You want to contrast the slow old process with the fast new one. How do you say it?
A "It is slow and it will be fast."
B "It used to be tedious, but now it is streamlined."
C "It would be tedious now."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada.

Record yourself comparing the past and the present at work in four sentences. Use "used to" for past habits and states, "would" for repeated past actions, and "but now" for the change.

Survival Card — Lesson 17

1I used to work on call, but now we have a rotation.Eu costumava ficar de plantão, mas agora temos um rodízio.
2Every night, the legacy system would crash.Toda noite, o sistema legado caia.
3The process used to be slow and tedious.O processo costumava ser lento e tedioso.
4We used to deploy manually, but now it is automated.Nós costávamos implantar manualmente, mas agora é automatizado.
5We did a full overhaul, and the outdated scripts are gone.Fizemos uma reformulação completa, e os scripts ultrapassados sumiram.
Aula 18 — Pre-class

Cause and Result (Connectors)

Ligue causa e resultado com conectores: because e since antes da causa, so / as a result / therefore antes do resultado — "The server was overloaded, so requests timed out".
0%
▼

Warm-up — Callback da Aula 17

Review

Na Aula 17 você aprendeu a comparar passado e presente com used to / would e "but now" ("We used to deploy manually, but now it is automated"). Agora você explica o porquê: liga uma causa ao seu resultado. Use because e since antes da causa, e so, as a result ou therefore antes do resultado. (Link the cause to its result, clearly.)

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra, leia o exemplo e a tradução. Toque em Listen para ouvir.

Latency -- the delay between a request and the response (PT: latência / atraso de resposta)
"Since the latency was high, requests timed out."
Capacity -- the maximum amount a system can handle (PT: capacidade / limite máximo)
"The server reached its capacity during the surge."
Threshold -- the limit at which something changes or triggers (PT: limiar / limite)
"Because the load passed the threshold, latency went up."
Overload -- to put more work on a system than it can handle (PT: sobrecarregar)
"The traffic surge overloaded the server."
Congestion -- a slowdown caused by too much traffic at once (PT: congestionamento)
"One failure caused congestion across the system."
Throttle -- to deliberately limit the number of requests (PT: limitar / restringir a vazão)
"We had to throttle the requests to protect the system."
Dependency -- something a system relies on to work (PT: dependência)
"The real cause was a dependency on a slow database."
Redundancy -- an extra backup that takes over if one part fails (PT: redundância / reserva)
"Since the database had no redundancy, one failure was enough."
Resilience -- the ability to keep working when something fails (PT: resiliência)
"Because we lacked resilience, a small overload became a big outage."
Consequence -- the direct result or effect of an action or event (PT: consequência / resultado)
"The consequence of the surge was a full outage."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a tradução em português.

Latency
Capacity
Threshold
Overload
Congestion
Throttle
Dependency
Redundancy
Resilience
Consequence

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

In the briefing, Daniela linked each cause to its result. "There was a surge in traffic, so the server reached its capacity," she said — so goes before the result. "Because the load passed the threshold, the latency went up" — because goes before the cause. As a result, requests timed out: as a result is a more formal way to introduce the effect. She also used since, which means the same as because: "Since there was no redundancy, one failure caused congestion." Remember: never use because and so together in the same sentence — choose one.

1. In "...so the server reached its capacity", what does "so" introduce?
A The result.
B The cause.
C The future.
2. What does "since" mean in this lesson?
A The same as "so".
B The same as "because" (it introduces the cause).
C A point in time only.
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "Because it was overloaded, so it crashed."
B "Because it was overloaded, it crashed."
C "The server was overloaded because the service crashed."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — Cause & Result Connectors

GRAMMAR

Conectores de causa e de resultado (bilingue EN + PT).

ConnectorIntroduzExample
because / sinceA causa (o motivo)"...because the server was overloaded."
soO resultado (informal)"...so the service crashed."
as a result / thereforeO resultado (formal)"As a result, the latency went up."
Regra (PT)Use because e since antes da CAUSA; use so, as a result e therefore antes do RESULTADO. "X happened because Y" = "Y, so X" (mesma ideia, ordem inversa). NUNCA use because e so juntos na mesma frase.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase completa.

"The service crashed the server was overloaded."
"The server was overloaded, the service crashed."
" the latency was high, requests timed out."
"The capacity was low. As a , the system slowed down."
"We had to the requests to protect the system."
"Because we lacked , a small overload became a big outage."

Stage 2: Build a Cause-and-Result Chain

Order

Coloque a cadeia de causa e resultado na ordem correta.

?As a result, requests timed out and there was congestion.
?There was a traffic surge, so the server reached its capacity.
?Since there was no redundancy, one failure spread everywhere.
?Because the load passed the threshold, the latency went up.
?The consequence was an outage, so we improved our resilience.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a com bom ritmo.

The service crashed because the server was overloaded.
O serviço caiu porque o servidor estava sobrecarregado.
The latency was high, so requests timed out.
A latência estava alta, então as requisições expiraram.
Since the database had no redundancy, one failure caused congestion.
Como o banco de dados não tinha redundância, uma falha causou congestionamento.
The capacity was low. As a result, the system slowed down.
A capacidade estava baixa. Como resultado, o sistema ficou lento.
Because we lacked resilience, a small overload became a big outage.
Como faltava resiliência, uma pequena sobrecarga virou uma grande queda.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação.

You want to state the result first, then the cause. Which is correct?
A "The service crashed because the server was overloaded."
B "The server was overloaded because the service crashed."
C "So the server was overloaded, the service crashed."
You want a formal way to introduce the result in a report. Which is best?
A "Because, the system slowed down."
B "As a result, the system slowed down."
C "Since, the system slowed down."
Which sentence mixes the connectors incorrectly?
A "Because it was overloaded, so it crashed."
B "It was overloaded, so it crashed."
C "It crashed because it was overloaded."
You explain why one failure spread. Which is correct?
A "So there was no redundancy, one failure spread."
B "Since there was no redundancy, one failure spread."
C "As a result there was no redundancy, one failure spread."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada.

Record yourself explaining a cause-and-result chain from a real problem at work, in four sentences. Use "because" or "since" for the cause, and "so" or "as a result" for the result.

Survival Card — Lesson 18

1The service crashed because the server was overloaded.O serviço caiu porque o servidor estava sobrecarregado.
2The latency was high, so requests timed out.A latência estava alta, então as requisições expiraram.
3Since the database had no redundancy, one failure caused congestion.Como o banco de dados não tinha redundância, uma falha causou congestionamento.
4The capacity was low. As a result, the system slowed down.A capacidade estava baixa. Como resultado, o sistema ficou lento.
5Because we lacked resilience, a small overload became a big outage.Como faltava resiliência, uma pequena sobrecarga virou uma grande queda.
Aula 19 — Pre-class

Softening Bad News (Hedging)

Dê uma notícia difícil com diplomacia: suavize o fato (it seems / there appears to be), o futuro (might / may) e o tamanho do problema (slight / minor) — "it seems the rollback caused a small delay".
0%
▼

Warm-up — Callback da Aula 18

Review

Na Aula 18 você aprendeu a ligar causa e resultado com because, so e as a result ("The server was overloaded, so requests timed out"). Agora você dá essa notícia ruim com tato. Use três ferramentas: suavize o fato com it seems / there appears to be; suavize o futuro com might / may / could; diminua o problema com slight / minor. Seja honesta, mas gentil. (Be honest, but gentle and diplomatic.)

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra, leia o exemplo e a tradução. Toque em Listen para ouvir.

Setback -- a problem that delays or reverses progress (PT: contratempo / revés)
"It seems we have hit a small setback."
Shortfall -- a gap between what you have and what you need (PT: déficit / falta)
"There is a minor shortfall in the budget."
Drawback -- a disadvantage or negative side of an option (PT: desvantagem / ponto negativo)
"The main drawback is a longer timeline."
Workaround -- a temporary solution that avoids the problem (PT: solução de contorno / paliativo)
"We have a workaround in place for now."
Fallback -- a backup option to use if the first one fails (PT: plano de reserva / alternativa)
"We have a temporary fallback in place."
Caveat -- a warning or condition that limits what you say (PT: ressalva / advertência)
"One caveat: these dates are still tentative."
Tentative -- not final or confirmed yet; provisional (PT: provisório / não confirmado)
"The launch date is still tentative."
Preliminary -- early or initial, before the final version (PT: preliminar / inicial)
"These are preliminary findings, not final."
Oversight -- a small mistake made by not noticing something (PT: descuido / falha por desatenção)
"There was an oversight in the planning."
Tradeoff -- a balance where you gain one thing but lose another (PT: troca / compensação)
"The tradeoff is more time for a safer launch."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a tradução em português.

Setback
Shortfall
Drawback
Workaround
Fallback
Caveat
Tentative
Preliminary
Oversight
Tradeoff

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

The direct version sounds harsh: "The project is late. The budget is short." Daniela softens it instead. First, she softens the fact: "It seems we have hit a small setback" and "There appears to be a slight delay." Second, she softens the future with modals: "We might need more time" and "We may need to revisit the numbers" — note that after might and may we use the base verb, never "to". Third, she shrinks the problem with words like slight and minor. She adds a caveat ("these figures are preliminary") and the workaround. The tone is gentle, but she stays clear and honest — she never hides the problem.

1. How does Daniela soften the fact that the project is late?
A She says "it seems" and "there appears to be".
B She hides the problem completely.
C She says it more directly.
2. What comes after "might" and "may"?
A "to" + verb
B the base verb, with no "to"
C the verb with -ing
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "We might to need more time."
B "We might need more time."
C "It seems is a small setback."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — Hedging & Diplomacy

GRAMMAR

As três ferramentas para suavizar (bilingue EN + PT).

ToolComoExample
Suavizar o fatoit seems / there appears to be"It seems we are a little behind."
Suavizar o futuromight / may / could (+ verbo base)"We might need more time."
Diminuir o problemaslight / minor / small / a little"It is a slight setback."
Regra (PT)Suavize o fato com it seems / there appears to be; suavize o futuro com might / may / could (+ verbo base, NUNCA "might to"); diminua o problema com slight / minor. Suavize o tom, mas mantenha a clareza — nunca esconda o problema, e ofereça o workaround.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase completa.

"It we have hit a small setback."
"There appears to be a slight delay, so we need more time."
"There is a minor in the budget."
"One : these dates are still tentative."
"It is a setback, not a disaster."
"There was an oversight, but we have a in place."

Stage 2: Build a Diplomatic Update

Order

Coloque as frases de um update diplomático na ordem correta.

?There is also a minor shortfall, so we may need to revisit the numbers.
?It seems we have hit a small setback on the rollout.
?One caveat: these figures are preliminary and the dates are tentative.
?There appears to be a slight delay, so we might need more time.
?But we have a workaround in place, and the tradeoff is a safer launch.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a com bom ritmo.

It seems we have hit a small setback.
Parece que tivemos um pequeno contratempo.
There appears to be a slight delay, so we might need more time.
Parece haver um pequeno atraso, então talvez precisemos de mais tempo.
There is a minor shortfall, so we may need to revisit the numbers.
Há um pequeno déficit, então talvez precisemos revisar os números.
One caveat: these figures are preliminary and the dates are tentative.
Uma ressalva: estes números são preliminares e as datas ainda são provisórias.
There was an oversight, but we have a workaround in place.
Houve um descuido, mas temos uma solução de contorno em vigor.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação.

The project is behind schedule. How do you soften the news?
A "It seems we are a little behind."
B "The project is a complete failure."
C "We are extremely late, sorry."
You think you will need more time, but you are not sure. Which is best?
A "We might to need more time."
B "We might need a little more time."
C "We will definitely fail the deadline."
You want to add a condition to your statement. Which is correct?
A "One caveat: these figures are preliminary."
B "These figures are final, no doubt."
C "There appear to be one caveat."
After softening the bad news, what should you add to reassure?
A "There is nothing we can do."
B "But we already have a workaround in place."
C "It is all going to fail anyway."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada.

Record yourself giving a difficult update diplomatically, in four sentences. Soften the fact with "it seems", the future with "might/may", and the problem with "slight/minor", then add the workaround.

Survival Card — Lesson 19

1It seems we have hit a small setback.Parece que tivemos um pequeno contratempo.
2There appears to be a slight delay, so we might need more time.Parece haver um pequeno atraso, então talvez precisemos de mais tempo.
3There is a minor shortfall, so we may need to revisit the numbers.Há um pequeno déficit, então talvez precisemos revisar os números.
4One caveat: these figures are preliminary and the dates are tentative.Uma ressalva: estes números são preliminares e as datas ainda são provisórias.
5There was an oversight, but we have a workaround in place.Houve um descuido, mas temos uma solução de contorno em vigor.
Aula 20 — Pre-class

The Executive Summary (Bringing It All Together)

Resuma um projeto para um líder ocupado: abra com o headline (in short / to sum up), dê um recap curto com signposting (first / then / overall) e feche com o takeaway e o next step — "in short, the launch is on track".
0%
▼

Warm-up — Callback da Aula 19

Review

Na Aula 19 você aprendeu a dar notícias ruins com diplomacia (it seems / might / a slight delay). Agora, na sua aula final, você junta tudo do ano em uma habilidade: o executive summary. Quatro partes: abra com o headline (in short / to sum up + o resultado); dê um recap curto com signposting (first / then / overall); dê o takeaway (the key takeaway is...); feche com o next step. Lidere com o resultado e seja concisa. (Headline first. Then the details.)

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra, leia o exemplo e a tradução. Toque em Listen para ouvir.

Summary -- a short version that gives only the main points (PT: resumo)
"Here is a quick summary of the project."
Headline -- the opening line that states the main result first (PT: manchete / frase de abertura)
"The headline is: the launch is on track."
Recap -- a quick review of the key points so far (PT: revisão rápida / recapitulação)
"Let me give a quick recap of the results."
Overview -- a general view of the whole, without details (PT: visão geral / panorama)
"First, a brief overview of the project."
Highlight -- the most important and positive point to share (PT: destaque / ponto alto)
"The main highlight is high adoption."
Takeaway -- the one main message you want people to remember (PT: conclusão principal / lição)
"The key takeaway: we are on schedule."
Outcome -- the final result of a project or action (PT: resultado final / desfecho)
"The outcome is a successful launch."
Concise -- short and clear, with no extra words (PT: conciso / breve e claro)
"Keep the summary concise and clear."
Brief -- short and to the point; a short summary (PT: breve / resumido)
"I will give you a brief update."
Gist -- the main idea or essential point of something (PT: essência / ideia principal)
"The gist is that we are on track."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a tradução em português.

Summary
Headline
Recap
Overview
Highlight
Takeaway
Outcome
Concise
Brief
Gist

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

A busy board has little time, so Daniela leads with the result. First, the headline: "In short, the launch is on track." Then a brief recap with signposting: "First, the system is live. Then, the team is trained. Overall, adoption is high." She keeps it concise — short and clear, never a long story. She softens the one risk with the diplomacy from last lesson ("it seems there is a minor delay, but we have a workaround"). Finally, she gives the takeaway ("the key takeaway is that we are on schedule") and the next step. Remember: the result goes first, and "a summary" needs the article — "Let me give you a summary", never "Let me give you summary".

1. Where does the headline go in a good summary?
A First, before the details.
B Last, after the long story.
C It does not matter.
2. Which words guide the listener through the recap?
A because / so
B first / then / overall
C might / may
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "Let me give you summary."
B "Let me give you a summary."
C "The takeaway are on schedule."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — The Executive Summary

GRAMMAR

As quatro partes de um summary (bilingue EN + PT).

PartComoExample
1. Headlinein short / to sum up + o resultado"In short, the launch is on track."
2. Recapfirst / then / overall + pontos-chave"First, it is live. Overall, adoption is high."
3. Takeawaythe key takeaway is..."The key takeaway is we are on schedule."
Regra (PT)Abra com o headline (resultado primeiro: in short / to sum up); dê um recap curto com signposting (first / then / overall); feche com o takeaway e o next step. Seja concisa e use o artigo: "a summary", "the takeaway".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase completa.

"In , the launch is on track."
"Let me give you a quick of the results."
"Overall, the main is high adoption."
"The key is that we are on schedule."
"Keep the summary and clear."
"The is that we are on track."

Stage 2: Build an Executive Summary

Order

Coloque as frases de um executive summary na ordem correta.

?Overall, the main highlight is high adoption.
?In short, the launch is on track.
?The key takeaway is that we are on schedule.
?First, the system is live. Then, the team is trained.
?The next step is the final review on Friday.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a com bom ritmo.

In short, the launch is on track.
Em resumo, o lançamento está dentro do prazo.
First, the system is live. Then, the team is trained.
Primeiro, o sistema está no ar. Depois, a equipe está treinada.
Overall, the main highlight is high adoption.
No geral, o principal destaque é a alta adoção.
The key takeaway is that we are on schedule.
A conclusão principal é que estamos dentro do cronograma.
The next step is the final review on Friday.
O próximo passo é a revisão final na sexta-feira.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação.

The board has only five minutes. How do you open your summary?
A "In short, the launch is on track."
B "First the server crashed, then we found a bug, then..."
C "Let me tell you the whole story from the beginning."
You want to guide the listener through three key points. Which is best?
A "Because, so, and as a result."
B "First,... Then,... Overall,..."
C "Maybe, perhaps, and possibly."
You want to end with the one message to remember. Which is correct?
A "The key takeaway is that we are on schedule."
B "The key takeaway are on schedule."
C "And that is everything in full detail."
Which sentence uses the article correctly?
A "Let me give you summary."
B "Let me give you a brief summary."
C "Let me give you brief overview no article."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada.

Record yourself giving an executive summary of a real project, in four sentences: headline, recap (with first/then/overall), takeaway and next step. Lead with the result and keep it concise.

Survival Card — Lesson 20

1In short, the launch is on track.Em resumo, o lançamento está dentro do prazo.
2First, the system is live. Then, the team is trained.Primeiro, o sistema está no ar. Depois, a equipe está treinada.
3Overall, the main highlight is high adoption.No geral, o principal destaque é a alta adoção.
4The key takeaway is that we are on schedule.A conclusão principal é que estamos dentro do cronograma.
5The next step is the final review on Friday.O próximo passo é a revisão final na sexta-feira.
Aulas 21–43

Content will be added progressively

New lessons will be released as Daniela progresses through the program.
▼

Content will be added progressively.

Parabéns, Daniela!

Você completou todos os exercícios pre-class disponíveis. Continue praticando!

Aula 01 — Who is Daniela?

Diagnostic + Personal Introduction — 60 min
TempoFaseAtividade
0–3Warm-upRapport building. Say: “Hi Daniela! Welcome to your first class. Before we start, tell me — how was your week?” Let her answer freely (even in Portuguese if needed). Observe fluency level.
3–5Warm-upAsk: “Have you ever been to an international event or conference?” Then: “What was the most challenging moment?” Note her vocabulary range and confidence level.
5–8Warm-upAsk: “If you met someone from Nike USA right now, what would you say?” This is the diagnostic moment — observe what she can already produce spontaneously.
8–10Warm-upSet expectations: “Today we are going to build your personal introduction. By the end of this class, you will be able to introduce yourself confidently at any professional event.”
10–13Pre-teachWord 1: introduction. Say: “Allow me to make a brief introduction.” Drill pronunciation: /In-tro-DUK-shn/. Daniela repeats 3x. Note: stress is on 3rd syllable, NOT “in-tro-du-TION”.
13–15Pre-teachWord 2: manage. Say: “She manages a team of 20 engineers.” Drill: /MAN-ij/. Watch for “ma-NA-ge” (Portuguese interference). Elicit: “What do you manage at SPF Group?”
15–17Pre-teachWord 3: based in. Say: “Our headquarters is based in downtown Sao Paulo.” Drill: /beIst In/ linked together. Watch for “bah-sed”. Always followed by “in” + place.
17–19Pre-teachWord 4: responsible for. Say: “He is responsible for the entire supply chain.” Drill: /ri-SPON-si-bl fr/. Schwa on “for” — it is NOT stressed. Common error: “I responsible for” (missing “am”).
19–21Pre-teachWord 5: background. Say: “Her background includes 15 years in technology.” Drill: /BAK-ground/. Elicit: “What is your background, Daniela?” Refers to past experience, NOT personality.
21–23Pre-teachWord 6: enjoy. Say: “I really enjoy solving complex problems.” Drill: /in-JOY/. Followed by -ing form. Common error: “I enjoy to work” instead of “I enjoy working.”
23–25Pre-teachQuick-fire review. Point to each word and Daniela says it + one phrase from memory. If she hesitates, prompt with the first word.
25–30TeachPresent Simple for personal descriptions. Board pattern: I + am/work/manage/live. “We use Present Simple for things that are always true about us.” Show contractions: I am → I'm.
30–35TeachDialogue model. Share dialogue on screen (Tab 4). Read together line by line. Daniela reads her lines. Teacher reads John's lines. Focus on natural delivery.
35–38TeachCCQ 1: “When do we use 'based in'?” Expected: “To say where we live or work.” CCQ 2: “Is 'background' about the past or the future?” Expected: “The past.” CCQ 3: “Do we say 'I have 44 years' or 'I am 44 years old'?” Expected: “I am 44 years old.”
38–40TeachSelf-Introduction Structure (share on screen): 1. Greeting + Name → 2. Job Title + Company → 3. Location → 4. Responsibilities → 5. Background / Interests.
40–48PracticeOral Drilling (8 prompts). Teacher says situation in PT, student responds in EN:
1. “Diga seu nome” → “Hi, I'm Daniela.”
2. “Diga seu cargo” → “I'm an IT Manager.”
3. “Diga onde você trabalha” → “I work at SPF Group.”
4. “Diga onde você mora” → “I'm based in Sao Paulo.”
5. “Diga o que a empresa faz” → “We distribute Centauro and Nike in Brazil.”
6. “Diga sua responsabilidade principal” → “I'm responsible for SAP implementation.”
7. “Diga sua formação” → “My background is in information technology.”
8. “Diga o que você gosta de fazer” → “I enjoy working with technology and people.”
48–52PracticeListening Comprehension. Play the dialogue (Tab 4). Daniela listens and answers 3 questions:
1. “What is John's job?”
2. “Where is Daniela based?”
3. “What company does Daniela work for?”
52–60PracticeError Correction. Show 5 sentences on screen (Tab 4). Daniela identifies and corrects each error:
1. “I have 44 years old.” → “I am 44 years old.”
2. “I work in the Centauro.” → “I work at Centauro.”
3. “I responsible for IT.” → “I am responsible for IT.”
4. “My background is in the information technology.” → “My background is in information technology.”
5. “I enjoy to work with people.” → “I enjoy working with people.”
60–68ProductionStage 1 — Guided. Daniela reads the model introduction from screen, filling in her own details. Teacher provides corrections in real time. Repeat 2x until smooth.
68–76ProductionStage 2 — Semi-free. Remove the model from screen. Give keyword prompts only (name, job, company, location, responsibility, background). Daniela produces her own introduction. Note errors for delayed feedback.
76–85ProductionStage 3 — Free role-play. Full simulation: Teacher is “John from Microsoft” at a conference. They shake hands, exchange intros, have 3 minutes of natural conversation. Delayed feedback after the role-play — note 3 positives and 2 areas for improvement.
85–87Wrap-up3 standard questions:
1. “What was the most useful thing you learned today?”
2. “What do you want to practice more?”
3. “On a scale of 1-10, how confident do you feel introducing yourself in English?”
87–90Wrap-upHomework assignment + preview. Assign homework. Say: “Next class we will learn how to break the ice and start conversations at events. You will love it!”

Detailed Teacher Guide Reference

Exact phrases to say:
- Opening: “Hi Daniela, welcome to Alumni! I am so happy to start this journey with you. Today is all about YOU — we are going to build your personal introduction in English.”
- Transition to vocab: “Before we practice, let me teach you some key words. These are the building blocks of any professional introduction.”
- Transition to practice: “Great! Now you know the words. Let us put them together. I am going to describe a situation in Portuguese, and you respond in English. Ready?”
- Transition to production: “You are doing amazing! Now let us do this for real. I am John from Microsoft. We just met at a conference.”
- Closing: “Daniela, you did incredibly well today. You can already introduce yourself in English! That is a huge step.”
Anticipated obstacles specific to Daniela:
- Freeze response: She may say “ai meu Deus” and switch to Portuguese when nervous. Solution: smile, say “It is okay! Take your time. Start with 'Hi, I am Daniela.'”
- Age expression: Will likely say “I have 44 years” (PT interference). Correct immediately: “In English we say 'I AM 44 years old.' We use BE, not HAVE for age.”
- Missing articles: “I am manager” instead of “I am A manager” or “I am AN IT Manager.” Correct gently each time.
- Preposition errors: “I work in the Centauro” instead of “I work AT Centauro.” Drill: AT + company name.
- Enjoy + to: “I enjoy to work” instead of “I enjoy working.” Drill pattern: enjoy + -ing.
Drilling notes per word:
- introduction /In-tro-DUK-shn/ — 4 syllables, stress on 3rd. Daniela may say /in-tro-du-SHON/ with Portuguese vowels.
- manage /MAN-ij/ — 2 syllables, stress on 1st. Watch for /ma-NA-je/ (3 syllables).
- based in /beIst In/ — linked sound, almost “base-din.” Watch for /BAH-sed/.
- responsible for /ri-SPON-si-bl fr/ — “for” is a schwa, almost silent. Never stressed.
- background /BAK-ground/ — compound word, stress on first part. Not “back-GROUND.”
- enjoy /in-JOY/ — stress on 2nd syllable. Always + gerund (-ing form).
CCQs with expected answers:
- “When do we use 'based in'?” → To say where we live or work.
- “What is the difference between 'manage' and 'lead'?” → Manage = processes and systems. Lead = people and vision.
- “Is 'background' about the past or the future?” → The past — your experience and education.
- “Do we say 'I have 44 years' or 'I am 44 years old'?” → I am 44 years old.
- “After 'enjoy', do we use 'to + verb' or '-ing'?” → -ing: I enjoy working.
Delayed feedback notes:
During the free role-play (Stage 3), do NOT interrupt. Take notes on:
- Pronunciation errors (write exact words)
- Grammar mistakes (write exact sentence)
- Fluency observations (long pauses, fillers, L1 use)
After the role-play, share: 3 positive points FIRST, then 2 areas for improvement. Always end on a positive.

Homework

  • Write a 5-8 sentence paragraph introducing yourself at a professional event
  • Record an audio of the paragraph (use pre-class P4 to practice)
  • Practice saying it 3 times without reading

O que eu aprendi — Aula 1

  • Self-introduction structure (name, job, company, city)
  • Present Simple for personal descriptions
  • Key vocabulary: manage, based in, responsible for, background
  • Oral drilling completed (8 prompts)
  • Error correction exercise completed
  • 60-second self-introduction recording completed
  • Free role-play completed with delayed feedback
  • Homework assigned and understood

Aula 02 — Breaking the Ice

First Contact at International Events — 60 min
TempoFaseAtividade
0–3Warm-upCallback from Lesson 1. Say: “Daniela, introduce yourself to me like we just met at a conference. Go!” Observe improvement since last class.
3–5Warm-upHomework review. Listen to her audio recording. Give 2 positives, 1 improvement area. Ask: “How did you feel recording yourself?”
5–8Warm-upAsk: “Tell me about your week. What was the best part?” Then: “Did you use any English this week?” Then: “What is the most difficult thing about talking to strangers?”
8–10Warm-upSet today's goal: “Today you are going to learn how to start conversations naturally. No more 'ai meu Deus' — you will have 5 ready-to-use openers!”
10–13Pre-teachWord 1: networking. Say: “Good networking can open many doors in your career.” Drill: /NET-work-ing/. Stress on 1st syllable. Elicit: “Do you do networking at events, Daniela?”
13–15Pre-teachWord 2: attendee. Say: “Each attendee received a name badge at registration.” Drill: /a-ten-DEE/. Stress on 3rd syllable. Not “attendant” (different meaning).
15–17Pre-teachWord 3: approach. Say: “She decided to approach the speaker after the talk.” Drill: /a-PROACH/. Physical action + communication. Elicit: “Is it easy to approach people at events?”
17–19Pre-teachWord 4: keynote. Say: “The CEO delivered a powerful keynote about innovation.” Drill: /KEE-note/. The most important speech at a conference. Compound word.
19–21Pre-teachWord 5: first time. Say: “Is this your first time at this conference?” Drill the whole question as a chunk. Natural conversation opener.
21–23Pre-teachWord 6: keep in touch. Say: “Here is my LinkedIn. Let us keep in touch.” Drill as a chunk. Phrasal verb — cannot separate. Used to end conversations warmly.
23–25Pre-teachQuick-fire review. Say the Portuguese, Daniela says the English. Speed round — all 6 words.
25–30TeachQuestion Tags. Pattern: positive statement + negative tag. “Great conference, isn't it?” “You work in tech, don't you?” Show 5 examples on screen. Say together.
30–35Teach5 Natural Openers. Share on screen (Tab 4). Read each one aloud. Discuss WHEN to use each (coffee break, after keynote, registration, etc.).
35–38TeachDialogue model. Share dialogue on screen (Tab 4). Read together. Focus on the FLOW: opener → response → introduction → questions → closing.
38–40TeachCCQ 1: “Is 'Great conference, isn't it?' a question or a statement?” Expected: “Both — it starts a conversation.” CCQ 2: “When do we use question tags?” Expected: “To confirm or start conversation.” CCQ 3: “What is the difference between 'approach' and 'talk to'?” Expected: “Approach = the physical movement of going near someone.”
40–48PracticeOral Drilling (8 prompts). Teacher says situation in PT:
1. “Você vê alguém sozinho no coffee break” → “Hi there! Great conference, isn't it?”
2. “Você quer saber se a pessoa já veio antes” → “Is this your first time at this conference?”
3. “Você quer saber por que a pessoa veio” → “What brings you to the conference?”
4. “Você acabou de assistir uma palestra incrível” → “That was an amazing keynote, wasn't it?”
5. “Você quer se apresentar” → “Hi, I don't think we've met. I'm Daniela.”
6. “Alguém pergunta o que você faz” → “I'm an IT Manager at SPF Group. We distribute Centauro and Nike.”
7. “Você quer devolver a pergunta” → “What about you? What do you do?”
8. “Você quer encerrar a conversa educadamente” → “It was great talking to you. Let's keep in touch!”
48–52PracticeListening Comprehension. Play the dialogue from Tab 4. Daniela listens and answers:
1. “Where does Lisa work?”
2. “What opener does Daniela use?”
3. “How do they end the conversation?”
52–60PracticeError Correction (5 sentences). Show on screen:
1. “Nice to meet you. What is your work?” → “What do you do?”
2. “Great conference, isn't?” → “Great conference, isn't it?”
3. “I am coming from Brazil.” → “I am from Brazil.”
4. “This is my first time in this conference.” → “This is my first time at this conference.”
5. “Let us keep the touch.” → “Let us keep in touch.”
60–68ProductionStage 1 — Guided. Scenario: Coffee break at SAP conference. Teacher gives step-by-step instructions: “First, approach me. Then, use an opener. Then, introduce yourself.” Daniela follows the steps with prompts.
68–76ProductionStage 2 — Semi-free. Scenario: Lunch table at conference. Teacher is “Lisa from Deloitte.” Daniela must approach, open conversation, exchange information, and close. Teacher gives minimal prompts.
76–85ProductionStage 3 — Free role-play. Scenario: After the keynote. Teacher is “James from AWS.” Full 5-minute conversation with NO prompts. Daniela must initiate, maintain, and close the conversation. Delayed feedback after.
85–87Wrap-up3 standard questions:
1. “Which opener do you like the most?”
2. “What was the hardest part of the conversation?”
3. “On a scale of 1-10, how confident do you feel starting a conversation in English?”
87–90Wrap-upHomework + preview. Assign homework. Say: “Next class we will talk about YOUR job in detail. You will build a job pitch that impresses anyone!”

Detailed Teacher Guide Reference

Exact phrases to say:
- Opening: “Last class you built your introduction. Today we are going to learn something even more important — how to START the conversation.”
- After vocab: “These words will help you navigate any networking event. Now let us see how to use them in real conversations.”
- Before role-play: “Imagine we are at an SAP conference in Orlando. I am a stranger standing near the coffee station. What do you do?”
- Key coaching phrase: “Remember: after you say something, always ask a question back. 'What about you?' is your best friend.”
Anticipated obstacles specific to Daniela:
- One-way conversation: Daniela may give long answers but forget to ask questions back. Teach the “What about you?” reflex.
- Panic with fast responses: When teacher responds quickly as “the American,” Daniela may freeze. Solution: speak slower at first, gradually increase speed.
- Literal translation of “né?”: May produce “isn't?” without “it.” Drill: “isn't IT?” “don't YOU?” always has a subject.
- Preposition “in/at”: “First time IN this conference” instead of “AT.” Drill: AT + events, AT + conferences.
Drilling notes per word:
- networking /NET-work-ing/ — stress on 1st syllable. Not “net-WORK-ing.” Compound word.
- attendee /a-ten-DEE/ — stress on last syllable. Not “attendant” (different word).
- approach /a-PROACH/ — stress on 2nd syllable. /pr/ blend — watch for inserted vowel.
- keynote /KEE-note/ — compound word, stress on 1st part. Not “key-NOTE.”
- first time — drill as a chunk: “Is this your FIRST TIME?” Rising intonation.
- keep in touch — phrasal verb, drill as a unit: /keep-in-TUCH/. Cannot separate.
Delayed feedback notes:
During free role-play, note:
- Did she use at least 2 different openers?
- Did she ask questions back? (What about you? Where are you from?)
- Did she close the conversation naturally?
- Body language observations (even on camera — eye contact, posture)
After: 3 positives first, then 2 improvements.

Homework

  • Listen to 2 podcast clips about small talk (Business English Pod)
  • Write 5 different conversation openers for a conference scenario
  • Practice saying each opener 3 times until natural

O que eu aprendi — Aula 2

  • Question tags (isn't it?, don't you?)
  • 5 natural conversation openers
  • Small talk flow: open → engage → close
  • Oral drilling completed (8 prompts)
  • Error correction exercise completed
  • 3-stage role-play completed
  • Confidence in approaching strangers
  • Homework assigned and understood

Aula 03 — Talking About Your Job

IT Manager at Centauro/Nike — 60 min
TempoFaseAtividade
0–3Warm-upCallback from Lesson 2. Say: “Daniela, you see me standing alone at a conference. Start a conversation!” She must approach, use an opener, and introduce herself. Quick check on Lessons 1-2 skills.
3–5Warm-upHomework review. Review her 5 openers. Read them together. Give feedback on naturalness and grammar.
5–8Warm-upAsk: “Describe your job in 10 words. Go!” Then: “Now in 5 words.” Then: “Now in 3 words.” This is a fun diagnostic to see how she simplifies.
8–10Warm-upAsk: “What is the hardest thing about explaining your job to someone who doesn't know SAP?” Set today's goal: “By the end of class, you will have 3 versions of your job pitch.”
10–13Pre-teachWord 1: oversee. Say: “The CTO oversees all digital transformation initiatives.” Drill: /o-ver-SEE/. Higher level than “manage.” Elicit: “What do you oversee at SPF Group?”
13–15Pre-teachWord 2: lead. Say: “She leads cross-functional teams across 3 countries.” Drill: /leed/ (present) vs /led/ (past). Common error: “I lead” (past) should be “I led.”
15–17Pre-teachWord 3: implement. Say: “The team successfully implemented the new ERP system.” Drill: /IM-pli-ment/. Stress on 1st syllable. Very common in IT context.
17–19Pre-teachWord 4: distribute. Say: “The company distributes products to over 200 stores.” Drill: /dis-TRIB-yoot/. Stress on 2nd syllable. Not “DIST-ri-bute.”
19–21Pre-teachWord 5: retail. Say: “The retail industry is rapidly adopting AI solutions.” Drill: /REE-tail/. Watch for /he-TAIL/ (Portuguese r interference). Stress on 1st syllable.
21–23Pre-teachWord 6: infrastructure. Say: “Cloud infrastructure has transformed how companies operate.” Drill: /IN-fra-struk-cher/. 4 syllables, stress on 1st. Complex word — practice slowly.
23–25Pre-teachQuick-fire review. Point to words, Daniela produces sentences. Focus on natural delivery, not just accuracy.
25–30TeachPresent Simple for job descriptions. Pattern: I + oversee/lead/manage/implement. Show structure on screen: Title + Company + What company does + Your responsibilities + Your team.
30–35Teach3 versions of job pitch. Share structure on screen (Tab 4):
10s: “I'm [title] at [company].”
30s: + what company does + location
2min: + responsibilities + team + background + interesting fact
35–38TeachDialogue model. Share dialogue on screen (Tab 4). Read together. Practice explaining SAP to a non-tech person.
38–40TeachCCQ 1: “When would you use the 10-second version?” Expected: “Elevator, quick intro.” CCQ 2: “When would you use the 2-minute version?” Expected: “Dinner, deep networking.” CCQ 3: “What is the difference between 'oversee' and 'manage'?” Expected: “Oversee = higher level/strategic, manage = hands-on/operational.”
40–48PracticeOral Drilling (8 prompts).
1. “Alguém pergunta: What do you do?” → “I'm an IT Manager at SPF Group.”
2. “Alguém pergunta: What does your company do?” → “We distribute Centauro and Nike products in Brazil.”
3. “Alguém pergunta: What is SAP?” → “SAP is an enterprise software that manages business operations.”
4. “Alguém pergunta: What are you responsible for?” → “I'm responsible for implementing SAP solutions for our retail operations.”
5. “Alguém pergunta: How big is your team?” → “I lead a team of 12 IT professionals.”
6. “Alguém pergunta: What do you oversee?” → “I oversee all technology projects in the company.”
7. “Explique seu trabalho para uma criança de 10 anos” → “I help a big sports store use computers to sell shoes and clothes better.”
8. “Faça seu pitch de 10 segundos” → “I'm an IT Manager at SPF Group, the company behind Centauro and Nike in Brazil.”
48–52PracticeListening Comprehension. Play the dialogue from Tab 4. Daniela listens and answers:
1. “What does Daniela's company do?”
2. “How does Daniela explain SAP?”
3. “What is Tom's reaction?”
52–60PracticeError Correction (5 sentences).
1. “I responsible for the IT department.” → “I am responsible for the IT department.”
2. “I oversee to all technology projects.” → “I oversee all technology projects.”
3. “We distribute the products for 200 stores.” → “We distribute products to over 200 stores.”
4. “I lead a team of 12 IT's professionals.” → “I lead a team of 12 IT professionals.”
5. “My company implement SAP solutions.” → “My company implements SAP solutions.”
60–68ProductionStage 1 — Guided. Scenario: Casual networking at conference lobby. Daniela uses 10-second pitch. Teacher prompts each step. Practice 3 times with timer visible.
68–76ProductionStage 2 — Semi-free. Scenario: Formal meeting with potential client. Daniela uses 30-second pitch. Teacher is “a VP from Nike USA” who asks follow-up questions.
76–85ProductionStage 3 — Free role-play. Scenario: Job interview. Teacher is HR at an international company. “Tell me about your current role.” Daniela uses 2-minute pitch + handles 3 follow-up questions. Delayed feedback after.
85–87Wrap-up3 standard questions:
1. “Which pitch version was the easiest for you?”
2. “How would you explain SAP to someone at a party?”
3. “On a scale of 1-10, how confident are you talking about your job in English?”
87–90Wrap-upHomework + preview. Assign homework. Say: “Next class is about articles and English rhythm. We are going to polish everything you learned so far!”

Detailed Teacher Guide Reference

Exact phrases to say:
- Opening: “You already know how to introduce yourself and start conversations. Today we go deeper — we are going to make people say 'Wow, that is interesting!' when you describe your job.”
- During pitch building: “Think of it like a sandwich. The 10-second version is just the bread. The 30-second version adds the filling. The 2-minute version is the full meal with sides.”
- Before production: “Forget that you are in a class. You are at a real conference. This person really wants to know about your work.”
Anticipated obstacles specific to Daniela:
- Too much detail in short version: Daniela is organized and thorough — she may try to say everything in 10 seconds. Use timer to keep her on track.
- Retail pronunciation: /REE-tail/ not /he-TAIL/. The Portuguese “r” will make this sound wrong. Drill extensively.
- Lead vs Led: Present /leed/ vs Past /led/. She may mix them up. Write both on screen with pronunciation guide.
- Infrastructure: Long word, may cause hesitation. Break it down: IN-fra-STRUC-ture. Practice slowly then speed up.
- SAP explanation: She knows SAP deeply but may not know how to simplify it in English. Help her find the “elevator version.”
Drilling notes per word:
- oversee /o-ver-SEE/ — stress on 3rd syllable. Different from “overlook” (which means to miss). Oversee = supervise from above.
- lead /leed/ (present) — rhymes with “seed.” /led/ (past) — rhymes with “bed.” Critical distinction.
- implement /IM-pli-ment/ — stress on 1st. The noun and verb have the same stress in American English.
- distribute /dis-TRIB-yoot/ — stress on 2nd. Not “DIST-ri-bute.”
- retail /REE-tail/ — stress on 1st. The /r/ must be American, not Portuguese /h/.
- infrastructure /IN-fra-struk-cher/ — 4 syllables. Stress on 1st. Very common in IT context.

Homework

  • Record 30-second job pitch, self-evaluate using criteria: clarity, confidence, pronunciation
  • Write all 3 versions (10s, 30s, 2min) and send to teacher
  • Practice the 10-second version until it feels completely natural

O que eu aprendi — Aula 3

  • 3 versions of job pitch (10s, 30s, 2min)
  • Job description vocabulary: oversee, lead, implement, distribute, retail, infrastructure
  • Present Simple for describing responsibilities
  • Oral drilling completed (8 prompts)
  • Error correction exercise completed
  • 3-stage role-play completed (casual, formal, interview)
  • 30-second pitch recorded and self-evaluated
  • Homework assigned and understood

Aula 04 — Articles and English Rhythm

Definite/Indefinite Articles + Pronunciation — 60 min
TempoFaseAtividade
0–3Warm-upCallback from Lesson 3. Say: “Daniela, give me your 10-second job pitch. Ready? Go!” Then: “Now the 30-second version.” Observe article usage and rhythm.
3–5Warm-upHomework review. Listen to her 30-second pitch recording. Focus feedback specifically on articles: “Did you notice you said 'I am manager'? In English we need 'a' or 'an' before job titles.”
5–8Warm-upAsk: “Tell me about your morning today. What did you do before this class?” While she speaks, note every article error. Then share: “I counted [X] places where you needed an article. Let us fix that today!”
8–10Warm-upAsk: “Do you know the difference between 'a' and 'the' in English?” Let her explain in her own words. Set today's goal: “After today, articles will never be a mystery again.”
10–14Pre-teachArticle rules overview (share on screen). A/AN: first mention, general, one of many. THE: specific, already mentioned, unique, superlatives. NO ARTICLE: plural general, uncountable general, proper nouns. AN before vowel SOUNDS: an IT manager, an hour, an MBA.
14–17Pre-teachWord 1: the (definite). Say: “The IT department is on the third floor.” The = we both know which one. Drill: /ð@/ before consonants (weak), /ði:/ before vowels (stronger).
17–19Pre-teachWord 2: a/an (indefinite). Say: “I need a new laptop for the project.” A/AN = one of many, first mention. AN before vowel SOUNDS (not letters): an hour, a university.
19–21Pre-teachWord 3: stress. Say: “The stress falls on the second syllable: im-PLE-ment.” Stress = the emphasis on one syllable. Content words get stress, function words don't.
21–23Pre-teachWord 4: rhythm. Say: “English has a different rhythm than Portuguese.” English = stress-timed. Portuguese = syllable-timed. This is why Brazilians sound “robotic” in English.
23–25Pre-teachWords 5-6: syllable, intonation. Drill: “The word technology has four syllables.” “Rising intonation at the end makes it a question.” Quick practice with examples.
25–30TeachGrammar: Article rules in depth. Share visual explanation on screen (Tab 4). Go through 6 rules with examples from Daniela's context (SPF Group, Centauro, SAP). Test each rule with a quick example.
30–35TeachRhythm: Stress-timed vs syllable-timed. Show sentences with stress marks on screen (Tab 4). Demonstrate: “I WORK at the SPF GROUP in Sao PAULO.” Tap desk for stressed syllables. Daniela joins tapping.
35–38TeachDialogue model. Share dialogue on screen (Tab 4). Read together focusing on articles and rhythm. Teacher exaggerates stress pattern, then normalizes.
38–40TeachCCQ 1: “Why do we say 'an IT Manager' not 'a IT Manager'?” Expected: “Because IT starts with a vowel sound /aI/.” CCQ 2: “Do we say 'I work at company' or 'I work at a company'?” Expected: “A company, first mention.” CCQ 3: “Which words do we stress?” Expected: “Nouns, verbs, adjectives, adverbs.”
40–48PracticeOral Drilling (8 prompts). Teacher reads sentence WITHOUT articles, Daniela adds them:
1. “I am ___ IT Manager at ___ SPF Group.” → “I am AN IT Manager at THE SPF Group.”
2. “We need ___ new server for ___ project.” → “We need A new server for THE project.”
3. “___ conference starts at 9 AM.” → “THE conference starts at 9 AM.”
4. “Centauro is ___ largest retailer in ___ Latin America.” → “Centauro is THE largest retailer in Latin America.”
5. “I have ___ meeting with ___ CEO tomorrow.” → “I have A meeting with THE CEO tomorrow.”
6. “She is ___ experienced manager.” → “She is AN experienced manager.”
7. “I enjoy working in ___ retail industry.” → “I enjoy working in THE retail industry.”
8. “SAP is ___ enterprise software.” → “SAP is AN enterprise software.”
48–52PracticeListening Comprehension. Teacher reads a paragraph about a company (Tab 4). Daniela listens and identifies: how many times was “the” used? How many times “a/an”? Were there zero-article cases?
52–60PracticeError Correction (5 sentences).
1. “I am manager at company.” → “I am a manager at the company.”
2. “I work in the retail industry in the Brazil.” → “I work in the retail industry in Brazil.”
3. “I need new laptop.” → “I need a new laptop.”
4. “The Centauro is a biggest retailer.” → “Centauro is the biggest retailer.”
5. “I have a experience in the information technology.” → “I have experience in information technology.”
60–68ProductionStage 1 — Guided. Share a paragraph with article gaps on screen (Tab 4). Daniela reads aloud, filling in articles. Focus on correct pronunciation of “the” and “a/an” in context.
68–76ProductionStage 2 — Semi-free. Rhythm exercise: Show 5 sentences with stress marks (Tab 4). Daniela reads with exaggerated stress, then normalizes. Record and compare with model. Tap along for rhythm.
76–85ProductionStage 3 — Free production. Say: “Describe your typical work day in 8-10 sentences. Focus on using articles correctly and speaking with natural rhythm.” Teacher notes errors for delayed feedback. Then: redo her 30-second pitch with corrected articles and rhythm.
85–87Wrap-up3 standard questions:
1. “What is the most confusing thing about articles?”
2. “Can you feel the difference in rhythm when you stress the right words?”
3. “On a scale of 1-10, how confident are you with articles now?”
87–90Wrap-upHomework + Netflix challenge. Assign homework. Say: “Your special challenge this week: watch one Netflix episode with English subtitles and notice the articles. You will be surprised how often 'the' and 'a' appear!”

Detailed Teacher Guide Reference

Exact phrases to say:
- Opening: “Today is special. We are not learning new conversation skills — we are POLISHING everything you already know. Articles and rhythm will make you sound 50% more natural.”
- Teaching articles: “Think of 'the' as a pointing finger. THE coffee = that specific coffee. A coffee = any coffee.”
- Teaching rhythm: “Portuguese is like a drum machine — every beat is the same. English is like jazz — some beats are strong, some are weak. Let us practice the jazz!”
- Closing: “Articles are one of the hardest things for Brazilian speakers. The fact that you are aware of them now is already a huge step.”
Anticipated obstacles specific to Daniela:
- Omitting articles: Portuguese allows dropping articles in many contexts where English requires them. Most common: “I am manager” (missing “a”).
- Overusing “the”: “I work in the Brazil” (countries don't use “the” unless plural: the US, the UK).
- Pronunciation of “the”: Always says /dee/ instead of /ð@/ (weak form). Practice the tongue-between-teeth /ð/ sound.
- Monotone rhythm: May give equal weight to every word. Use hand-tapping technique to mark stress.
Drilling notes per concept:
- the — weak form /ð@/ before consonants: “the BOOK”. Strong form /ði:/ before vowels: “the APPLE.”
- a/an — a = before consonant SOUNDS. an = before vowel SOUNDS. It is about SOUND not spelling: “a university” (starts with /j/), “an hour” (starts with /a/).
- stress /stres/ — single syllable, /s/ cluster. May add vowel: “es-TRESS.” Drill without the initial vowel.
- rhythm /RI-ð@m/ — silent “h.” The /ð/ sound again. Two syllables, stress on 1st.
- syllable /SIL-@-bl/ — 3 syllables, stress on 1st. Not “sy-LA-ble.”
- intonation /in-to-NAY-sh@n/ — 4 syllables, stress on 3rd.

Homework

  • Record 10 sentences about your work using correct articles
  • Change Netflix subtitles to English and watch 1 episode — notice articles
  • Re-record your 30-second pitch with corrected articles and better rhythm

O que eu aprendi — Aula 4

  • Definite vs. indefinite articles (a/an/the)
  • When to use zero article
  • Article gap-fill exercise completed
  • Sentence stress patterns
  • Rhythm drilling with stress marks
  • Error correction exercise completed
  • Natural English rhythm practice
  • Homework assigned and understood

Aula 05 — Describing Your Company

Centauro, Nike Brazil & SPF Group — 60 min
TempoFaseAtividade
0–3Warm-upCallback from Lesson 4. Say: “Daniela, tell me about your work day. I want to hear correct articles and good rhythm!” Listen for article improvements.
3–5Warm-upHomework review. Review her 10 recorded sentences. Focus on articles. Share: “Your articles are much better! I noticed [specific improvement].”
5–8Warm-upAsk: “Did you watch a Netflix episode with English subtitles? What did you notice about articles?” Then: “Describe Centauro to me like I have never heard of it.”
8–10Warm-upAsk: “If a foreign investor asked you about SPF Group, what would you say?” Let her try. Set goal: “Today you will build a company presentation that impresses investors.”
10–13Pre-teachWord 1: headquartered. Say: “Apple is headquartered in Cupertino, California.” Drill: /HED-kwor-terd/. 3 syllables, stress on 1st. Different from “based in” (headquartered = main office, based in = where you are).
13–15Pre-teachWord 2: revenue. Say: “Global revenue exceeded 2 billion dollars last year.” Drill: /REV-en-yoo/. 3 syllables. Not “income” (revenue = total sales, income = profit after costs).
15–17Pre-teachWord 3: market leader. Say: “Amazon is the market leader in e-commerce worldwide.” Drill as a compound: /MAR-kit LEE-der/. The company in first place.
