17–19Pre-teachWord 4: supply chain. Say: “A strong supply chain is essential for retail success.” Drill: /su-PLY chain/. The system of producing and delivering products. Very common in retail/tech.
19–21Pre-teachWord 5: subsidiary. Say: “Instagram is a subsidiary of Meta Platforms.” Drill: /sub-SID-ee-er-ee/. 5 syllables! Stress on 2nd. A company owned by another company.
21–23Pre-teachWord 6: the largest. Say: “Walmart is the largest employer in the United States.” Drill superlative pattern: THE + adjective-EST or THE MOST + adjective. Always with THE.
23–25Pre-teachQuick-fire review. Teacher says definition, Daniela says word. Then reverse: teacher says word, Daniela uses it in a sentence about SPF Group/Centauro.
25–30TeachSuperlatives review. Short adjectives: the largest, the biggest. Long adjectives: the most important, the most successful. Irregular: the best, the worst. Show on screen with examples from Daniela's context.
30–35TeachCompany description structure (share on screen). Template: 1. Company name & type → 2. Headquarters → 3. What it does → 4. Market position/size → 5. Your role. Practice with a model company first.
35–38TeachDialogue model. Share dialogue on screen (Tab 4). Read together: Daniela presents company to foreign investor. Focus on structure and superlatives.
38–40TeachCCQ 1: “What is the difference between 'headquartered' and 'based in'?” Expected: “Headquartered = the main office location. Based in = where you personally work.” CCQ 2: “When do we use 'the largest' vs 'larger'?” Expected: “Largest = comparing 3+, larger = comparing 2.” CCQ 3: “What is a subsidiary?” Expected: “A company owned by another company.”
40–48PracticeOral Drilling (8 prompts).
1. “Descreva o tamanho da sua empresa” → “Centauro is the largest sportswear retailer in Latin America.”
2. “Diga onde fica a sede” → “SPF Group is headquartered in Sao Paulo.”
3. “Descreva o que a empresa faz” → “We distribute Centauro and Nike products across Brazil.”
4. “Fale sobre o faturamento” → “The company generates significant annual revenue.”
5. “Explique a relação Centauro/SBF Group” → “Centauro is a subsidiary of the SBF Group.”
6. “Fale sobre a cadeia de suprimentos” → “We manage the entire supply chain for sportswear in Brazil.”
7. “Diga a posição de mercado” → “Centauro is the market leader in sportswear retail in Brazil.”
8. “Descreva seu papel na empresa” → “I oversee all IT operations including SAP implementation.”
48–52PracticeListening Comprehension. Play the dialogue from Tab 4. Daniela listens and answers:
1. “What is the investor interested in?”
2. “How many stores does Centauro have?”
3. “What makes Centauro different from competitors?”
52–60PracticeError Correction (5 sentences).
1. “Centauro is the more big retailer in Brazil.” → “Centauro is the biggest retailer in Brazil.”
2. “SPF Group is headquartered at Sao Paulo.” → “SPF Group is headquartered in Sao Paulo.”
3. “The company have significant revenue.” → “The company has significant revenue.”
4. “Centauro is a subsidiary from SBF Group.” → “Centauro is a subsidiary of the SBF Group.”
5. “We are the most largest retailer.” → “We are the largest retailer.”
60–68ProductionStage 1 — Guided. Scenario: Investor pitch. Teacher provides the structure template on screen. Daniela fills in each section with her own content about SPF Group/Centauro. Read together.
68–76ProductionStage 2 — Semi-free. Scenario: Conference panel. Teacher removes template. Daniela presents from memory with keyword prompts only. Teacher asks 3 follow-up questions as audience member.
76–85ProductionStage 3 — Free role-play. Scenario: LinkedIn bio read-aloud. Daniela writes 4-5 sentences about herself and her company (like a LinkedIn summary). She reads it aloud with natural rhythm. Then: full investor Q&A with no prompts. Delayed feedback after.
85–87Wrap-up3 standard questions:
1. “What is the most impressive thing about your company that you can say in English now?”
2. “Which word was hardest to pronounce?”
3. “On a scale of 1-10, how confident are you presenting your company in English?”
87–90Wrap-upHomework + celebration. Assign homework. Say: “You have completed the first 5 lessons! You can now introduce yourself, start conversations, describe your job, use articles correctly, and present your company. That is AMAZING progress!”

Detailed Teacher Guide Reference

Exact phrases to say:
- Opening: “Today is the last piece of the puzzle. You already know how to introduce yourself and start conversations. Now you are going to learn how to talk about your COMPANY with pride and confidence.”
- During teaching: “Imagine you are at a conference dinner. The person next to you is a VP from Nike USA. They ask: 'Tell me about your company.' What do you say?”
- Before production: “You are not just describing a company. You are telling a STORY. Centauro is the largest sportswear retailer in Latin America — that is impressive! Own it!”
- Closing: “Five lessons in, and look at you! You can introduce yourself, start conversations, describe your job, and present your company. That is real progress, Daniela.”
Anticipated obstacles specific to Daniela:
- Headquartered pronunciation: Complex word. Break down: HEAD + QUAR + TERED. Practice each part, then combine. She may say “head-QUAR-ter-ed” with wrong stress.
- Revenue vs Income: May confuse the two. Revenue = total money coming in. Income = what is left after costs. Use analogy: revenue is the whole pie, income is your slice.
- Information overload: Daniela knows a lot about SPF Group and may try to say everything. Use the template to keep her structured: What → Where → Size → My Role.
- Superlative errors: “the more big” or “the most largest” (double superlative). Drill: short adjective + EST, long adjective + MOST. Never both.
- Subsidiary pronunciation: 5 syllables! /sub-SID-ee-er-ee/. May simplify to 3 syllables. Practice slowly.
Drilling notes per word:
- headquartered /HED-kwor-terd/ — 3 syllables. Stress on 1st. The /kw/ blend is tricky for PT speakers.
- revenue /REV-en-yoo/ — 3 syllables. Stress on 1st. Not /re-VEN-ue/.
- market leader /MAR-kit LEE-der/ — compound noun, each word has stress on 1st syllable.
- supply chain /su-PLY chain/ — stress on 2nd syllable of “supply,” then “chain.”
- subsidiary /sub-SID-ee-er-ee/ — 5 syllables. Stress on 2nd. Practice slowly!
- the largest /ð@ LAR-jest/ — always with THE. Superlatives require THE.

Homework

  • Write company bio (6-8 sentences) about SPF Group/Centauro + audio recording
  • Write a 4-sentence LinkedIn summary in English
  • Practice presenting your company to a mirror for 2 minutes

O que eu aprendi — Aula 5

  • Company description vocabulary: headquartered, revenue, market leader, supply chain, subsidiary
  • Superlatives (the largest, the most important)
  • Company presentation structure (What, Where, Size, Role)
  • Oral drilling completed (8 prompts)
  • Error correction exercise completed
  • Investor Q&A simulation completed
  • LinkedIn bio written and read aloud
  • Homework assigned and understood

Aula 06 — SAP World

Talking About Systems and Technology — 60 min
TempoFaseAtividade
0–5Warm-upCallback Aula 5. Ask: “Last class we talked about your company. Can you describe SPF Group in 3 sentences?” Listen for superlatives and company vocabulary. Then: “What technology does your company use every day?”
5–10Warm-upAsk: “If a colleague from Germany asked you to explain SAP, what would you say?” This is the diagnostic moment. Set expectations: “Today we will learn to explain SAP workflows in English.”
10–26Pre-teach8 words: implement, module, workflow, database, integrate, customize, deploy, upgrade. Drill pronunciation for each. Elicit usage from Daniela's real work context. Quick-fire review at the end.
26–35TeachPresent Simple for processes. Pattern: Subject + verb(s). “SAP processes data. The module tracks payments.” Negative: “does not + verb.” Question: “Does SAP integrate?” CCQs to confirm understanding.
35–50PracticeOral Drilling + Error Correction. 8 prompts for producing tech descriptions. Then 5 error sentences for correction (missing -s, wrong form, etc.).
50–80Production3 stages: Guided (read model), Semi-free (keyword prompts only), Free (role-play: explain SAP to a new Nike USA colleague visiting the office).
80–87Wrap-upDelayed feedback. Share 3-4 errors from production. Daniela self-corrects.
87–90Wrap-upHomework: Write a glossary of 10 SAP terms in English with example sentences.

Aulas 07–43

Content will be added progressively.

Anotações do Professor

Lesson 1 — Who is Daniela?

Screen-share Content — Personal Introduction

Warm-up 10 min

1. How was your week at work?

2. Tell me something interesting that happened recently.

3. If you could travel anywhere this weekend, where would you go?

4. Have you ever been to an international conference?

5. If you met someone from Nike USA right now, what would you say?

Vocabulary 15 min

DIFFERENT examples from pre-class. Show on screen — no translations visible.

WordExample SentenceAudio
introduction“Allow me to make a brief introduction.”
manage“She manages a team of 20 engineers.”
based in“Our headquarters is based in downtown São Paulo.”
responsible for“He is responsible for the entire supply chain.”
background“Her background includes 15 years in technology.”
enjoy“I really enjoy solving complex problems.”

Grammar/Structure — Present Simple for Introductions 15 min

SELF-INTRODUCTION STRUCTURE:

1. Greeting + Name — “Hi, I am Daniela.”

2. Job Title + Company — “I am an IT Manager at SPF Group.”

3. Location — “I am based in Sao Paulo, Brazil.”

4. Responsibilities — “I am responsible for SAP implementation.”

5. Background / Interests — “My background is in information technology.”

MODEL (say together):

“Hello, I am Sarah. I am a Marketing Director at GlobalTech. I am based in New York. I am responsible for brand strategy and digital campaigns. My background is in communications.”

Dialogue — Meeting John at a Conference Reference

Context: Daniela meets John from Microsoft at an SAP conference in Orlando during the coffee break.

JOHN
John — Microsoft
Hi there! I am John, from Microsoft. What brings you to the conference?
DAN
Daniela
Hi John! Nice to meet you. I am Daniela Feitoza. I am an IT Manager at SPF Group.
JOHN
John
SPF Group? I am not familiar with that name. What does your company do?
DAN
Daniela
We distribute Centauro and Nike products in Brazil. Centauro is the largest sportswear retailer in Latin America.
JOHN
John
Wow, that is impressive! And you are based in Brazil?
DAN
Daniela
Yes, I am based in Sao Paulo. I am responsible for SAP implementation and IT infrastructure.
JOHN
John
That sounds great. How long have you been with the company?
DAN
Daniela
My background is in information technology, and I have been in this field for over 15 years. I really enjoy working with technology and people.

Listening Comprehension 5 min

After playing the dialogue, ask these questions (no answers visible to student):

1. What is John's job?

2. Where is Daniela based?

3. What company does Daniela work for?

Oral Drilling Prompts 8 min

Display on screen. Teacher reads the situation in Portuguese, student responds in English.

1. “Imagine you are at a conference. Someone asks your name.”

Expected: “Hi, I am Daniela.”

2. “Tell them your job title.”

Expected: “I am an IT Manager at SPF Group.”

3. “Say where you work.”

Expected: “I am based in Sao Paulo, Brazil.”

4. “Explain what your company does.”

Expected: “We distribute Centauro and Nike in Brazil.”

5. “Describe your main responsibility.”

Expected: “I am responsible for SAP implementation and IT infrastructure.”

6. “Talk about your experience.”

Expected: “My background is in information technology.”

7. “Say something you enjoy about your work.”

Expected: “I enjoy working with technology and people.”

8. “Give your complete 10-second introduction.”

Expected: “Hi, I am Daniela. I am an IT Manager at SPF Group.”

Error Correction 8 min

Share on screen. Daniela identifies and corrects each error.

1. “I have 44 years old.”

Correct: “I am 44 years old.”

2. “I work in the Centauro.”

Correct: “I work at Centauro.”

3. “I responsible for IT.”

Correct: “I am responsible for IT.”

4. “My background is in the information technology.”

Correct: “My background is in information technology.”

5. “I enjoy to work with people.”

Correct: “I enjoy working with people.”

Production — Role-Play Scenarios 25 min

Scenario 1 — Coffee Break

You are at the coffee station during an SAP conference in Orlando. John from Microsoft approaches you and says “Hi there!” Introduce yourself and have a 2-minute conversation.

Scenario 2 — Dinner Table

You are seated at a dinner table with international colleagues. The person next to you is from Nike USA. They ask “So, tell me about yourself.” Give your full introduction.

Scenario 3 — Elevator Pitch

You are in an elevator with the CEO of a company you admire. You have 30 seconds. Give your best introduction before the door opens.

Substitution Drill Reference

Say the sentence, then change the underlined part.

“I am an IT Manager.” → Now say it with “Project Lead”

Expected: “I am a Project Lead.” (note: a, not an)

“I am based in Sao Paulo.” → Now say it with “New York”

Expected: “I am based in New York.”

“I manage the IT department.” → Now say it with “marketing team”

Expected: “I manage the marketing team.”

“I enjoy working with technology.” → Now say it with “traveling to conferences”

Expected: “I enjoy traveling to conferences.”

Lesson 2 — Breaking the Ice

Screen-share Content — Small Talk at Events

Warm-up 10 min

1. Introduce yourself to me like we just met at a conference. (Callback Lesson 1)

2. Tell me about your week. What was the best part?

3. Did you use any English this week outside of class?

4. What is the most difficult thing about talking to strangers?

5. If you saw someone standing alone at a conference, what would you do?

Vocabulary 15 min

DIFFERENT examples from pre-class. Show on screen — no translations visible.

WordExample SentenceAudio
networking“Good networking can open many doors in your career.”
attendee“Each attendee received a name badge at registration.”
approach“She decided to approach the speaker after the talk.”
keynote“The CEO delivered a powerful keynote about innovation.”
first time“This is my first time attending an international conference.”
keep in touch“Here is my LinkedIn. Let us keep in touch.”

Grammar/Structure — Question Tags + Openers 15 min

QUESTION TAG PATTERN:

Positive statement + negative tag = confirmation question

“Great conference, isn't it?” (is + not = isn't)

“You work in tech, don't you?” (do + not = don't)

“The keynote was amazing, wasn't it?” (was + not = wasn't)

5 NATURAL OPENERS:

1. “Great conference, isn't it?”

2. “Is this your first time at this event?”

3. “What brings you to the conference?”

4. “That was an amazing keynote, wasn't it?”

5. “Hi, I don't think we've met. I'm Daniela.”

Dialogue — Meeting Lisa at SAP Conference Reference

Context: Daniela approaches someone standing alone at the coffee station during an SAP conference.

DAN
Daniela
Hi there! Great conference, isn't it?
LISA
Lisa — Deloitte
It really is! I am really enjoying the sessions. I am Lisa, by the way.
DAN
Daniela
Nice to meet you, Lisa! I am Daniela, from Brazil. I work at SPF Group.
LISA
Lisa
Oh, Brazil! That is so cool. Is this your first time at this conference?
DAN
Daniela
Yes, it is my first time. I am an IT Manager and we use SAP for our retail operations. What about you?
LISA
Lisa
I work at Deloitte. We do SAP consulting for large companies. What brings you to the conference?
DAN
Daniela
I am here to learn about new SAP solutions for retail. Our company distributes Centauro and Nike in Brazil.
LISA
Lisa
Wow, Centauro! I know that brand. That is really interesting. We should definitely keep in touch!
DAN
Daniela
Absolutely! Here is my LinkedIn. It was great talking to you, Lisa. Let us keep in touch!

Listening Comprehension 5 min

1. Where does Lisa work?

2. What opener does Daniela use?

3. How do they end the conversation?

Oral Drilling Prompts 8 min

1. “You see someone alone at the coffee break.”

Expected: “Hi there! Great conference, isn't it?”

2. “You want to know if they came before.”

Expected: “Is this your first time at this conference?”

3. “You want to know their reason for attending.”

Expected: “What brings you to the conference?”

4. “You just watched an incredible keynote.”

Expected: “That was an amazing keynote, wasn't it?”

5. “You want to introduce yourself.”

Expected: “Hi, I don't think we've met. I'm Daniela.”

6. “Someone asks what you do. Respond and ask back.”

Expected: “I'm an IT Manager at SPF Group. What about you?”

7. “You want to return the question.”

Expected: “What about you? What do you do?”

8. “End the conversation politely.”

Expected: “It was great talking to you. Let's keep in touch!”

Error Correction 8 min

1. “Nice to meet you. What is your work?”

Correct: “Nice to meet you. What do you do?”

2. “Great conference, isn't?”

Correct: “Great conference, isn't it?”

3. “I am coming from Brazil.”

Correct: “I am from Brazil.”

4. “This is my first time in this conference.”

Correct: “This is my first time at this conference.”

5. “Let us keep the touch.”

Correct: “Let us keep in touch.”

Production — Role-Play Scenarios 25 min

Scenario 1 — Coffee Break

You are at the coffee station during an SAP conference. You see someone standing alone looking at their phone. Approach them, start a conversation using one of the 5 openers, introduce yourself, and exchange information.

Scenario 2 — Lunch Table

You sit down at a round table during the conference lunch. There are 3 other people you don't know. Start a group conversation. Introduce yourself and ask about others.

Scenario 3 — After the Keynote

The keynote just ended. You are walking out and someone next to you says “That was really good!” Continue the conversation for 3 minutes. Use at least 2 different openers/follow-up techniques.

Substitution Drill Reference

“Great conference, isn't it?” → Now change to “event”

Expected: “Great event, isn't it?”

“Is this your first time at this conference?” → Now change to “workshop”

Expected: “Is this your first time at this workshop?”

“What brings you to the conference?” → Now change to “Sao Paulo”

Expected: “What brings you to Sao Paulo?”

“Let us keep in touch!” → Now add “Here is my LinkedIn.” before it

Expected: “Here is my LinkedIn. Let us keep in touch!”

Lesson 3 — Talking About Your Job

Screen-share Content — Job Pitch

Warm-up 10 min

1. Start a conversation with me like we are at a conference. (Callback Lesson 2)

2. Describe your job in 10 words. Now 5. Now 3.

3. What is the hardest thing about explaining SAP to someone who doesn't know it?

4. What is the most interesting part of your job?

5. If a child asked you “What do you do?”, how would you answer?

Vocabulary 15 min

DIFFERENT examples from pre-class. Show on screen — no translations visible.

WordExample SentenceAudio
oversee“The CTO oversees all digital transformation initiatives.”
lead“She leads cross-functional teams across 3 countries.”
implement“The team successfully implemented the new ERP system.”
distribute“The company distributes products to over 200 stores.”
retail“The retail industry is rapidly adopting AI solutions.”
infrastructure“Cloud infrastructure has transformed how companies operate.”

Grammar/Structure — Job Pitch Structure 15 min

3 VERSIONS OF YOUR JOB PITCH:

10 seconds: “I am an IT Manager at SPF Group.”

30 seconds: + “We distribute Centauro and Nike products in Brazil. I am based in Sao Paulo.”

2 minutes: + Responsibilities + Team size + Background + SAP expertise + Interesting fact

PRESENT SIMPLE FOR JOB DESCRIPTIONS:

I oversee all technology projects.

I lead a team of 12 IT professionals.

We implement SAP solutions for retail.

The company distributes products to over 200 stores. (3rd person = -s)

Dialogue — Explaining Your Job to a Non-Tech Person Reference

Context: Daniela is at a networking dinner. Tom, a marketing director, asks about her work.

TOM
Tom — Marketing Director
So Daniela, what exactly do you do?
DAN
Daniela
I am an IT Manager at SPF Group. We distribute Centauro and Nike products in Brazil.
TOM
Tom
Oh interesting! What does an IT Manager do exactly?
DAN
Daniela
I oversee all technology projects in the company. My main focus is SAP implementation.
TOM
Tom
SAP? I have heard of it but I am not sure what it does. Can you explain?
DAN
Daniela
Sure! SAP is enterprise software that helps companies manage their operations. Think of it as the brain of the company — it connects sales, inventory, finance, everything.
TOM
Tom
That makes sense! How big is your team?
DAN
Daniela
I lead a team of 12 IT professionals. We are responsible for the entire IT infrastructure.
TOM
Tom
That is a big responsibility. I am impressed!

Listening Comprehension 5 min

1. What does Daniela's company do?

2. How does Daniela explain SAP to Tom?

3. What is Tom's reaction?

Oral Drilling Prompts 8 min

1. “Someone asks: What do you do?”

Expected: “I am an IT Manager at SPF Group.”

2. “Someone asks: What does your company do?”

Expected: “We distribute Centauro and Nike products in Brazil.”

3. “Someone asks: What is SAP?”

Expected: “SAP is enterprise software that manages business operations.”

4. “Someone asks: What are you responsible for?”

Expected: “I am responsible for implementing SAP solutions.”

5. “Someone asks: How big is your team?”

Expected: “I lead a team of 12 IT professionals.”

6. “Explain your job to a 10-year-old.”

Expected: “I help a big sports store use computers to sell shoes and clothes better.”

7. “What do you oversee?”

Expected: “I oversee all technology projects in the company.”

8. “Give your 10-second pitch.”

Expected: “I am an IT Manager at SPF Group, the company behind Centauro and Nike in Brazil.”

Error Correction 8 min

1. “I responsible for the IT department.”

Correct: “I am responsible for the IT department.”

2. “I oversee to all technology projects.”

Correct: “I oversee all technology projects.” (no preposition after oversee)

3. “We distribute the products for 200 stores.”

Correct: “We distribute products to over 200 stores.”

4. “I lead a team of 12 IT's professionals.”

Correct: “I lead a team of 12 IT professionals.” (no apostrophe)

5. “My company implement SAP solutions.”

Correct: “My company implements SAP solutions.” (3rd person -s)

Production — Role-Play Scenarios 25 min

Scenario 1 — Casual Networking

You are at an SAP conference lobby. Someone asks “What do you do?” Give your 10-second pitch, then expand naturally as they ask follow-up questions.

Scenario 2 — Formal Meeting

You are meeting a VP from Nike USA. They want to understand your role in the IT operations. Give your 30-second pitch and answer 3 follow-up questions professionally.

Scenario 3 — Job Interview

You are interviewing for an international IT Director position. The interviewer says “Tell me about your current role.” Give your full 2-minute pitch with confidence.

Substitution Drill Reference

“I oversee all technology projects.” → Now change to “digital transformation initiatives”

Expected: “I oversee all digital transformation initiatives.”

“I lead a team of 12 IT professionals.” → Now change to “8 developers”

Expected: “I lead a team of 8 developers.”

“We implement SAP solutions for retail.” → Now change to “cloud solutions for logistics”

Expected: “We implement cloud solutions for logistics.”

“We work in the retail industry.” → Now change to “tech industry”

Expected: “We work in the tech industry.”

Lesson 4 — Articles and English Rhythm

Screen-share Content — Articles + Pronunciation

Warm-up 10 min

1. Give me your 10-second job pitch. Now your 30-second version. (Callback Lesson 3)

2. Tell me about your morning today. What did you do before this class?

3. Do you know the difference between “a” and “the” in English?

4. Did you watch anything in English this week?

5. Say this sentence: “I am a manager at the company.” Which words feel important?

Vocabulary 15 min

DIFFERENT examples from pre-class. Focus on concepts, not just words.

WordExample SentenceAudio
the (definite)“The CEO approved the new IT budget yesterday.”
a/an (indefinite)“We hired an experienced SAP consultant last month.”
stress“In English, content words receive the main stress in a sentence.”
rhythm“English rhythm is like music, with strong and weak beats.”
syllable“The word infrastructure has four syllables.”
intonation“Your intonation should go up at the end of a yes or no question.”

Grammar/Structure — Article Rules 15 min

WHEN TO USE EACH ARTICLE:

A/AN — First mention, general, one of many
“I work at a company in Sao Paulo.” (any company, first mention)

THE — Specific, already mentioned, unique, superlatives
“The company distributes Nike products.” (specific company, already known)

NO ARTICLE — Plural general, uncountable general, proper nouns
“I work in information technology.” (general field, no article)

AN before vowel SOUNDS — an IT manager, an hour, an MBA
(It is about SOUND, not spelling: “a university” because /j/ sound)

DANIELA'S CONTEXT EXAMPLES:

“I am an IT Manager at the SPF Group.”

“Centauro is the largest retailer in Latin America.” (superlative = the)

“I work in __ Brazil.” (no article with country names)

“I need a new server for the project.” (a = first mention, the = specific)

Dialogue — Article Practice in Context Reference

Context: Daniela describes her work to a colleague. Every article is important.

“I work at the SPF Group. It is a large distributor based in Sao Paulo. The company distributes Centauro and Nike products across Brazil. I am an IT Manager, and I oversee the entire technology infrastructure. We use SAP, which is an enterprise software platform. The IT department has a team of 12 professionals. I enjoy working in the retail industry because it is always changing.”

Oral Drilling — Article Gap-Fill 8 min

Teacher reads sentence without articles. Student fills in the correct one.

1. “I am ___ IT Manager at ___ SPF Group.”

AN IT Manager / THE SPF Group

2. “We need ___ new server for ___ project.”

A new server / THE project

3. “___ conference starts at 9 AM.”

THE conference

4. “Centauro is ___ largest retailer in ___ Latin America.”

THE largest / no article (Latin America)

5. “I have ___ meeting with ___ CEO tomorrow.”

A meeting / THE CEO

6. “She is ___ experienced manager.”

AN experienced manager

7. “I enjoy working in ___ retail industry.”

THE retail industry

8. “SAP is ___ enterprise software.”

AN enterprise software

Error Correction 8 min

1. “I am manager at company.”

Correct: “I am a manager at the company.”

2. “I work in the retail industry in the Brazil.”

Correct: “I work in the retail industry in Brazil.” (no “the” with countries)

3. “I need new laptop.”

Correct: “I need a new laptop.”

4. “The Centauro is a biggest retailer.”

Correct: “Centauro is the biggest retailer.”

5. “I have a experience in the information technology.”

Correct: “I have experience in information technology.” (uncountable, no article)

Production — Rhythm Drilling 25 min

Stressed words are in CAPITALS. Tap desk for each stressed word. Practice exaggerated, then natural.

“I WORK at the SPF GROUP in Sao PAULO.”

“We are the LARGEST SPORTSWEAR RETAILER in Latin AMERICA.”

“I am RESPONSIBLE for SAP IMPLEMENTATION and IT INFRASTRUCTURE.”

“My BACKGROUND is in INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY.”

“I LEAD a TEAM of TWELVE IT PROFESSIONALS.”

Substitution Drill Reference

“I am an IT Manager.” → Now change to “engineer”

Expected: “I am an engineer.” (still “an” because /e/ vowel sound)

“I work at the SPF Group.” → Now change to “a startup”

Expected: “I work at a startup.” (general, not specific)

“The conference starts at 9 AM.” → Now change to “meeting”

Expected: “The meeting starts at 9 AM.”

“Centauro is the largest retailer.” → Now change to “most successful”

Expected: “Centauro is the most successful retailer.”

Lesson 5 — Describing Your Company

Screen-share Content — Company Presentation

Warm-up 10 min

1. Tell me about your work day. Use correct articles! (Callback Lesson 4)

2. Did you watch a Netflix episode with English subtitles? What did you notice?

3. Describe Centauro to me like I have never heard of it.

4. What makes SPF Group special compared to other companies?

5. If a foreign investor asked about your company, what would you say first?

Vocabulary 15 min

DIFFERENT examples from pre-class. Show on screen — no translations visible.

WordExample SentenceAudio
headquartered“Apple is headquartered in Cupertino, California.”
revenue“Global revenue exceeded 2 billion dollars last year.”
market leader“Amazon is the market leader in e-commerce worldwide.”
supply chain“A strong supply chain is essential for retail success.”
subsidiary“Instagram is a subsidiary of Meta Platforms.”
the largest“Walmart is the largest employer in the United States.”

Grammar/Structure — Superlatives + Company Description 15 min

SUPERLATIVE RULES:

Short adjectives (1-2 syllables): the + adjective + EST

“the largest” / “the biggest” / “the fastest”

Long adjectives (3+ syllables): the + MOST + adjective

“the most important” / “the most successful”

Irregular: the best / the worst / the most / the least

COMPANY DESCRIPTION TEMPLATE:

1. Name + Type: “SPF Group is a retail distribution company.”

2. Headquarters: “It is headquartered in Sao Paulo, Brazil.”

3. What it does: “We distribute Centauro and Nike products across Brazil.”

4. Market position: “Centauro is the largest sportswear retailer in Latin America.”

5. Your role: “I am responsible for SAP implementation and IT infrastructure.”

Dialogue — Presenting to a Foreign Investor Reference

Context: Daniela is at a business dinner. An investor from the US asks about her company.

INV
Investor — US Fund
I am very interested in the Brazilian retail market. Tell me about your company.
DAN
Daniela
I work at SPF Group. We are a retail distribution company headquartered in Sao Paulo. Our main brand is Centauro, which is the largest sportswear retailer in Latin America.
INV
Investor
Impressive! How many stores does Centauro have?
DAN
Daniela
We have over 200 stores across Brazil. We also distribute Nike products through our supply chain. The company generates significant annual revenue.
INV
Investor
And what makes Centauro different from other retailers?
DAN
Daniela
Centauro is the market leader in sportswear retail in Brazil. We are the only retailer with an exclusive Nike partnership. Our technology infrastructure, including SAP, is one of the most advanced in the industry.
INV
Investor
That is very compelling. What is your role in all of this?
DAN
Daniela
I am the IT Manager. I oversee all technology projects and I am responsible for SAP implementation. I lead a team of 12 IT professionals.

Listening Comprehension 5 min

1. What is the investor interested in?

2. How many stores does Centauro have?

3. What makes Centauro different from competitors?

Oral Drilling Prompts 8 min

1. “Describe the size of your company.”

Expected: “Centauro is the largest sportswear retailer in Latin America.”

2. “Say where the headquarters is.”

Expected: “SPF Group is headquartered in Sao Paulo.”

3. “Describe what the company does.”

Expected: “We distribute Centauro and Nike products across Brazil.”

4. “Talk about the revenue.”

Expected: “The company generates significant annual revenue.”

5. “Explain the relationship between Centauro and SBF Group.”

Expected: “Centauro is a subsidiary of the SBF Group.”

6. “Talk about the supply chain.”

Expected: “We manage the entire supply chain for sportswear in Brazil.”

7. “State the market position.”

Expected: “Centauro is the market leader in sportswear retail in Brazil.”

8. “Describe your role in the company.”

Expected: “I oversee all IT operations including SAP implementation.”

Error Correction 8 min

1. “Centauro is the more big retailer in Brazil.”

Correct: “Centauro is the biggest retailer in Brazil.”

2. “SPF Group is headquartered at Sao Paulo.”

Correct: “SPF Group is headquartered in Sao Paulo.”

3. “The company have significant revenue.”

Correct: “The company has significant revenue.” (3rd person singular)

4. “Centauro is a subsidiary from SBF Group.”

Correct: “Centauro is a subsidiary of the SBF Group.”

5. “We are the most largest retailer.”

Correct: “We are the largest retailer.” (never double superlative)

Production — Role-Play Scenarios 25 min

Scenario 1 — Investor Pitch

A US investor asks: “Tell me about your company.” Present SPF Group/Centauro using the template: Name, Headquarters, What it does, Market position, Your role. Handle 3 follow-up questions.

Scenario 2 — Conference Panel

You are on a panel at an SAP conference. The moderator says: “Daniela, can you tell the audience about your company and how you use SAP?” Present for 2 minutes.

Scenario 3 — LinkedIn Bio Read-Aloud

Write a 4-5 sentence LinkedIn summary about yourself and your company. Read it aloud with natural rhythm and correct articles. Then close your eyes and say it from memory.

Substitution Drill Reference

“Centauro is the largest retailer.” → Now change to “most innovative”

Expected: “Centauro is the most innovative retailer.”

“SPF Group is headquartered in Sao Paulo.” → Now change to “Rio de Janeiro”

Expected: “SPF Group is headquartered in Rio de Janeiro.”

“We distribute Nike products.” → Now change to “Adidas”

Expected: “We distribute Adidas products.”

“The company generates significant revenue.” → Now change to “impressive annual growth”

Expected: “The company generates impressive annual growth.”

Aulas 07–43

Content will be added progressively.

Notas da Aula

Atividades Complementares

Filmes, series, podcasts e recursos para acelerar seu progresso.

Aulas 1-2: Apresentacao Profissional & Networking

Vocabulario das aulas: introduction, manage, based in, responsible for, background, networking, attendee, approach, keynote, keep in touch.

Filme
The Intern (2015) — Primeiros 20 minutos

Ben (Robert De Niro) se apresenta para a equipe: "I'm Ben. I'm responsible for..." e faz networking com Jules. Preste atencao em como ele usa "background", "manage" e "based in" para descrever a carreira dele.

Dica: Anote 3 frases onde Ben se apresenta profissionalmente. Compare com o padrao da Aula 1: "I'm Daniela. I manage the IT department."
Serie
Silicon Valley — Season 1, Episode 1: "Minimum Viable Product"

Richard faz networking no TechCrunch Disrupt e apresenta o projeto dele. Preste atencao em como os attendees se aproximam (approach) e trocam contatos: "Let's keep in touch." Usa vocabulario identico ao da Aula 2.

Dica: Identifique 3 momentos de networking. Em cada um, anote como a pessoa se apresenta e qual frase de "keep in touch" usa.
Podcast
Business English Pod — Episode BEP 171: "Networking at a Conference (Part 1)"

Episodio especifico sobre networking em conferencias. Ensina exatamente como "approach" novas pessoas, fazer "introduction" e encerrar com "Let's keep in touch". O contexto da Aula 2.

Dica: Ouva o episodio e pratique as frases: "Is this your first time at this conference?" e "Here's my LinkedIn".
Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
TED Talk — Simon Sinek: "How Great Leaders Inspire Action"

Sinek abre com uma "introduction" poderosa, explica o "background" dele e fala sobre o que e "responsible for". Exatamente o padrao da Aula 1. Preste atencao na entonacao e no ritmo.

Dica: Assista os primeiros 5 minutos com legendas em ingles. Anote como Sinek se apresenta e compare com "Allow me to make a brief introduction" da aula.
Watch on YouTube ↗

Aulas 3-5: IT, Empresa & Supply Chain

Vocabulario das aulas: oversee, lead, implement, distribute, retail, infrastructure, headquartered, revenue, market leader, supply chain, subsidiary.

Documentario
The Social Dilemma (Netflix) — Primeiros 30 minutos

Ex-executivos de tech descrevem o que "oversee" e "lead" nas empresas deles. Falam de "infrastructure" e "implement" sistemas. Vocabulario tech identico ao das Aulas 3 e 5.

Dica: Anote 5 termos tech que ouvir (implement, infrastructure, database). Compare com o vocabulario da Aula 3: "I oversee all technology projects."
Podcast
SAP Community Podcast — Episode: "Retail Transformation with SAP S/4HANA"

Episodio sobre transformacao digital no varejo (retail) com SAP. Usa os termos exatos das Aulas 5 e 6: supply chain, retail, implement, module, workflow. Contexto SPF Group/Centauro.

Dica: Ouva prestando atencao em "supply chain", "retail" e "implement". Anote 3 frases e tente reescrever usando o contexto Centauro/Nike.
Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
SAP Learning — "What is SAP? A Beginner's Guide" (Official Channel)

Video oficial de 8 minutos que explica SAP usando "module", "workflow", "integrate", "customize" e "deploy". Exatamente o vocabulario da Aula 6.

Dica: Assista e anote como eles explicam cada modulo. Depois, pratique: "We implement SAP modules across all departments" usando o contexto SPF Group.
Watch on YouTube ↗

Aula 4: Artigos & Ritmo do Ingles + Pratica Geral

Vocabulario da aula: the (definite), a/an (indefinite), stress, rhythm, syllable, intonation.

Podcast
6 Minute English — BBC, Episode: "English: pair of words"

Episodio sobre ritmo e stress do ingles. Explica como a entonacao muda o significado, conectando diretamente com "stress", "rhythm" e "intonation" da Aula 4.

Dica: Ouva e repita as palavras prestando atencao na silaba tonica: im-PLE-ment, tech-NO-lo-gy. 6 minutos por dia.
Listen on Spotify ↗
Serie
The Office (US) — Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

Michael Scott se apresenta e descreve o escritorio. Usa "the" e "a" o tempo todo: "This is the office", "I'm a regional manager". Perfeito para praticar artigos definidos e indefinidos da Aula 4.

Dica: Nos primeiros 10 minutos, conte quantas vezes Michael usa "the" vs "a/an". Anote 3 exemplos de cada.
YouTube
Rachel's English — "English Rhythm and Stress: How to Sound Natural"

Rachel ensina exatamente o conceito de "stress" e "rhythm" da Aula 4. Mostra como a silaba tonica muda a pronuncia de palavras como "implement", "infrastructure" e "technology".

Dica: Pratique as 6 palavras da Aula 4 com o metodo da Rachel: bata palmas na silaba tonica de cada uma.
Watch on YouTube ↗
Filme
The Devil Wears Prada (2006) — Cena da entrevista (primeiros 15 min)

Andy (Anne Hathaway) se apresenta para Miranda Priestley usando "introduction", "background" e "responsible for". Linguagem corporativa identica as Aulas 1 e 3. Preste atencao no ritmo e na entonacao.

Dica: Assista a cena da entrevista e anote como Andy descreve a experiencia dela. Compare com: "My background is in information technology."

Aula 07: Past Simple — Telling Stories That Connect

Conteudos para reforcar o past simple e a arte de contar historias profissionais. Marque como concluido ao terminar.

Serie
Suits — Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

Mike Ross conta a historia de como chegou ao escritorio de Harvey Specter. O episodio inteiro e narrado no past simple: "I walked in", "She asked me", "We started working together". Perfeito para ouvir storytelling profissional real.

Dica: Nos primeiros 15 minutos, anote 10 verbos no past simple (regulares e irregulares). Depois, tente recontar a historia do Mike em 5 frases usando past simple.
Podcast
How I Built This (NPR) — Episode: "Spanx: Sara Blakely"

Sara Blakely conta como criou a Spanx do zero. Todo o episodio usa past simple profissional: "I decided", "We launched", "The company grew". E exatamente a estrutura de storytelling que voce praticou na Aula 07.

Dica: Ouca os primeiros 20 minutos e anote 5 verbos regulares e 5 irregulares no passado. Depois, conte sua propria historia profissional usando a mesma estrutura.
Listen on NPR ↗
YouTube
TED Talk — Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie: "The Danger of a Single Story"

Chimamanda usa past simple magistralmente para contar historias pessoais e profissionais: "I grew up", "I wrote my first story", "I discovered that". Cada historia conecta com a proxima, mostrando como storytelling profissional funciona na pratica.

Dica: Assista os primeiros 10 minutos com legendas em ingles. Anote como Chimamanda estrutura cada historia: comeco (contexto) + meio (o que aconteceu) + fim (resultado). Tente contar um projeto da Centauro usando a mesma estrutura.
Watch on YouTube ↗

Aula 08 — Networking in Action: Keeping the Conversation Going

Conteúdos para reforçar follow-up questions, escuta ativa e a arte de manter uma conversa. Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Series
Ted Lasso (Apple TV+) — Season 1, Episode 1

Ted Lasso is a master of small talk and curiosity. Watch how he meets new people, asks follow-up questions, finds something in common, and shows genuine interest in everyone he talks to. Direct connection to Lesson 8: keeping a conversation alive with warmth and good questions.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Anote 3 follow-up questions que o Ted faz quando conhece alguém novo.

Podcast
How to Be a Better Human — "How to make small talk count"

A friendly TED podcast episode about turning awkward small talk into real connection. The hosts share simple techniques for asking better follow-up questions and showing genuine interest. Perfect for Daniela’s international networking goals.

Dica: ouça os primeiros 20 minutos. Anote 3 perguntas que mantêm a conversa viva e tente usá-las na próxima conversa.

YouTube
TED — Celeste Headlee: "10 ways to have a better conversation"

A practical and famous talk on how to listen actively, ask open follow-up questions, and stay genuinely curious about the other person. Every tip maps directly onto Lesson 8: how to keep any conversation going without dominating it.

Dica: assista o vídeo completo (12 min). Pratique repetir 3 follow-up questions abertas que ela recomenda.

Aula 09 — Pronunciation Lab I: Sounds That Trip Up Brazilian Speakers

Conteúdos para reforçar o som do TH, a terminação -ed e os pares mínimos de vogais. Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Series
The Crown (Netflix) — any episode

A series with very clear, careful pronunciation. Focus your ear on the th sound and the -ed endings in past tense verbs. Slow, formal speech makes it easy to copy the sounds from Lesson 9.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Pause em 5 verbos no passado e repita o som -ed (sem vogal extra).

Podcast
The English We Speak (BBC) — short pronunciation episodes

Short, friendly episodes (3 to 5 minutes) with clear models of difficult sounds. Perfect for practicing the th sound and minimal pairs like ship and sheep at your own pace.

Dica: ouça um episódio e repita cada frase em voz alta logo depois (shadowing).

YouTube
Rachel’s English — "The TH Sound" and "-ED Endings"

Clear tutorials that show exactly where the tongue goes for the th sound and how the three -ed endings work. Maps directly onto Lesson 9, with slow-motion mouth views.

Dica: assista os dois vídeos e pratique no espelho, olhando a posição da língua.

Aula 10 — Pronunciation Lab II: Word Stress & Connected Speech

Conteúdos para reforçar o stress das palavras, as contrações e a fala conectada. Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Series
Ted Lasso (Apple TV) — any episode

Fast, natural, conversational English full of contractions and linking. Listen for how the strong words stand out and the small words almost disappear — exactly the rhythm from Lesson 10.

Dica: escolha uma cena curta, repita as falas em voz alta (shadowing) e copie o ritmo.

Podcast
Pronuncian / American English Pronunciation — rhythm episodes

Short episodes that explain word stress, weak forms, and linking with clear examples. Perfect for hearing how content words are pushed and function words are reduced.

Dica: ouça um episódio e bata o ritmo com a mão nas palavras fortes.

YouTube
Rachel’s English — "Word Stress" and "Linking" lessons

Clear tutorials on sentence stress, the schwa, contractions, and how to link words. Maps directly onto Lesson 10, with slow examples you can copy out loud.

Dica: assista os dois vídeos e pratique uma frase do trabalho com o ritmo certo.

Aula 11 — Present Perfect vs Past Simple: Talking About Experience

Conteúdos para reforçar a diferença entre present perfect e past simple. Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Series
The Office (US) — any job interview or review scene

Characters constantly talk about their careers and experience. Listen for "I have worked here for..." (experience) versus "I started in..." (exact time) — exactly the contrast from Lesson 11.

Dica: anote uma frase no present perfect e uma no past simple de cada cena.

Podcast
Luke’s English Podcast — episodes on the present perfect

Clear, friendly explanations of when to use the present perfect and when to use the past simple, with many real examples about life and work experience.

Dica: pause e repita cada exemplo, dizendo por que aquele tempo foi escolhido.

YouTube
BBC Learning English — "Present Perfect vs Past Simple"

A short, clear lesson on the difference between the two tenses, with the signal words ever, never, already, yet, just, and since. Maps directly onto Lesson 11.

Dica: depois do vídeo, descreva sua carreira em 5 frases usando os dois tempos.

Aula 12 — Retail & Fashion Tech Vocabulary: Presenting a Project

Conteúdos para reforçar o vocabulário de tecnologia de varejo e a apresentação de projetos. Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Series
Silicon Valley — any product demo or pitch scene

The characters constantly demo products and pitch to stakeholders. Listen for tech terms like platform, scalable, user interface, and rollout — exactly the vocabulary from Lesson 12.

Dica: anote três termos técnicos compostos (noun + noun) de cada cena.

Podcast
Shopify Masters — episodes on retail technology

Real retail and e-commerce founders talk about their inventory systems, payment gateways, checkout flows, and analytics. Hear the exact vocabulary from Lesson 12 used in real business.

Dica: pause e repita cada termo composto, dizendo o que descreve o que.

YouTube
"How to give a product demo to stakeholders" (presentation skills)

A short, clear video on structuring a product walkthrough and naming each part of a system. Maps directly onto Daniela’s stakeholder meeting in Lesson 12.

Dica: depois do vídeo, apresente um projeto de varejo em 5 frases usando os termos da aula.

Aula 13 — Expressing Opinions Professionally

Conteúdos para reforçar como dar opiniões com confiança e tato — frases de opinião, hedging e softeners. Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Series
The Office (US) — any conference room meeting scene

The team constantly shares opinions and disagrees in meetings. Listen for phrases like "in my view", "I see your point, but", and softeners like "maybe" and "a little" — the exact language from Lesson 13.

Dica: anote três frases usadas para discordar com respeito.

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — episodes where guests debate a business decision

Experts give opinions, hedge when uncertain, and disagree politely with the host. Hear how professionals say "from my perspective", "it might", and "that is a valid point" in real discussions.

Dica: pause e repita cada softener (might, perhaps, a little) que você ouvir.

YouTube
"How to disagree politely in English" (business English lesson)

A short, clear video on opinion phrases, hedging language and softeners for meetings. Maps directly onto Daniela’s debate with Marcus in Lesson 13.

Dica: depois do vídeo, dê a sua opinião sobre uma decisão do trabalho em 4 frases, suavizando uma delas.

Aula 14 — Talking About the Future

Conteúdos para reforçar como falar de planos, previsões e compromissos — going to, will e present continuous. Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Series
Silicon Valley — any product roadmap or launch-planning scene

The team constantly plans launches, predicts results and sets deadlines. Listen for "we are going to...", "it will...", and present continuous for scheduled meetings — the exact forms from Lesson 14.

Dica: anote uma frase com going to, uma com will e uma com present continuous.

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — episodes about forecasting and project planning

Leaders talk about roadmaps, forecasts and what they intend to do. Hear how professionals announce plans ("we are going to"), make predictions ("it will") and describe deliverables and contingencies.

Dica: pause e repita cada previsão com "will" que você ouvir.

YouTube
"Will vs going to vs present continuous for the future" (English lesson)

A short, clear video on the three future forms and when to use each. Maps directly onto Daniela’s pilot planning with Marcus in Lesson 14.

Dica: depois do vídeo, descreva o plano de um projeto seu em 4 frases, usando as três formas.

Aula 15 — Setting the Scene (Past Continuous)

Conteúdos para reforçar como narrar um incidente — past continuous para a cena, past simple para o evento. Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Series
Mr. Robot — any server outage or incident-response scene

The characters narrate what was happening when systems went down. Listen for the scene-and-surprise pattern: "I was monitoring... when it crashed" — the exact structure from Lesson 15.

Dica: anote uma frase com was/were + -ing e o evento em past simple que a interrompe.

Podcast
The Downtime Project — engineers tell the story of real outages

Each episode walks through an incident: what the team was doing, what suddenly broke, how they recovered and the root cause. A perfect model of the past continuous setting the scene.

Dica: pause e repita cada frase com "while we were..." que você ouvir.

YouTube
"Past Continuous vs Past Simple — when and while" (English lesson)

A short, clear video on using the past continuous for the scene and the past simple for the interrupting event. Maps directly onto Daniela’s outage review with Marcus in Lesson 15.

Dica: depois do vídeo, narre um incidente seu em 4 frases, usando past continuous e past simple.

Aula 16 — Sequencing the Past (Past Perfect)

Conteúdos para reforçar como ordenar uma história — past perfect para a ação mais antiga, sequenciadores para a ordem. Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Series
The Crowded Room or any whodunit — a scene that reconstructs a timeline

Investigators piece together what had happened before a key moment. Listen for the past perfect ("she had already left") and sequencers (first, then, after that) — the exact tools from Lesson 16.

Dica: anote uma frase com "had + past participle" e diga qual ação aconteceu primeiro.

Podcast
The Downtime Project — postmortem episodes with full timelines

Engineers reconstruct exactly what had happened before each step of an incident. A great model of the past perfect plus sequencers, used to narrate a real postmortem in order.

Dica: pause e repita cada frase com "by the time..." ou "had already..." que você ouvir.

YouTube
"Past Perfect explained — had + past participle for the earlier action" (English lesson)

A short, clear video on using the past perfect for the earlier of two past events, plus sequencers to order a story. Maps directly onto Daniela’s postmortem with Marcus in Lesson 16.

Dica: depois do vídeo, narre o timeline de um incidente seu em 4 frases, usando o past perfect.

Aula 17 — Comparing Then and Now (used to / would)

Conteúdos para reforçar como comparar passado e presente — used to / would para o passado, but now para o presente. Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Series
Halt and Catch Fire — characters reflect on how tech and teams used to work

The show spans years of tech change. Listen for characters saying how things "used to be" and what they "would do" in the early days — the exact forms from Lesson 17.

Dica: anote uma frase com "used to" e uma com "would", e diga qual descreve um estado.

Podcast
The Changelog — episodes where engineers compare old and new practices

Developers describe how teams used to deploy manually, fight fires and carry a backlog, and how automation changed everything. A great model of used to, would and "but now".

Dica: pause e repita cada frase com "used to" ou "would" que você ouvir.

YouTube
"Used to vs Would — talking about the past" (English lesson)

A short, clear video on used to for habits and states, would for repeated actions, and the contrast with the present. Maps directly onto Daniela’s then-and-now talk with Marcus in Lesson 17.

Dica: depois do vídeo, compare uma rotina sua de antes e de agora em 4 frases.

Aula 18 — Cause and Result (Connectors)

Conteúdos para reforçar como ligar causa e resultado — because/since para a causa, so/as a result para o resultado. Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Series
Air Crash Investigation — any episode explaining a chain of cause and effect

Investigators link each cause to its result step by step. Listen for "because", "since", "so" and "as a result" connecting the chain — the exact connectors from Lesson 18.

Dica: anote uma frase com "because" e uma com "as a result", e diga qual parte é a causa.

Podcast
The Downtime Project — engineers explain the cause and result of outages

Each episode breaks down why an outage happened and what each cause led to. A great model of cause-and-result connectors used to brief a technical audience.

Dica: pause e repita cada frase com "so" ou "because" que você ouvir.

YouTube
"Linking words: because, so, since, as a result" (English lesson)

A short, clear video on cause-and-result connectors and the order of cause and result. Maps directly onto Daniela’s briefing with Marcus in Lesson 18.

Dica: depois do vídeo, explique uma cadeia de causa e resultado sua em 4 frases.

Aula 19 — Softening Bad News (Hedging)

Conteúdos para reforçar como dar notícias difíceis com diplomacia — it seems, might/may, slight/minor. Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Series
The Diplomat — any scene where a character softens difficult news

Diplomats deliver hard messages gently. Listen for "it seems", "there appears to be", "we might" and softening words — the exact hedging tools from Lesson 19.

Dica: anote uma frase suavizada e diga qual tool foi usado (it seems / might / slight).

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — episodes on delivering difficult feedback and bad news

Leaders discuss how to break bad news while staying honest and constructive. A great model of hedging language and diplomatic framing for a professional update.

Dica: pause e repita cada frase com "it seems" ou "we might" que você ouvir.

YouTube
"Hedging language: how to be diplomatic in English" (Business English lesson)

A short, clear video on softening statements with "it seems", modals and softening adjectives. Maps directly onto Daniela’s rehearsed client update with Marcus in Lesson 19.

Dica: depois do vídeo, suavize 4 frases de notícia ruim do seu trabalho.

Aula 20 — The Executive Summary (Bringing It All Together)

Conteúdos para reforçar como resumir um projeto com clareza — headline first, signposting (first/then/overall) e o takeaway. Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Series
Billions — any boardroom or briefing scene where a leader summarizes a deal

Executives open with the result and keep it short. Listen for "in short", "the headline is", "the bottom line" and "the takeaway" — the exact summary tools from Lesson 20.

Dica: anote a frase de abertura (headline) e o takeaway de uma cena.

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — episodes on communicating with executives and leading with the point

Leaders explain how to brief a busy executive: headline first, keep it concise, end with one takeaway. A perfect model of executive summary language for your board reports.

Dica: ao final, resuma o próprio episódio em 3 frases (headline, recap, takeaway).

YouTube
"How to give an executive summary" (Business English / presentation skills)

A short, clear video on structuring a summary: headline first, recap with signposting, then the takeaway and next step. Maps directly onto Daniela’s board rehearsal with Marcus in Lesson 20.

Dica: depois do vídeo, grave um executive summary de 1 minuto de um projeto seu.
